arguments & your request. I have made enquiries about getting the Saddlery [strikethrough: things] made, find they Can be done partly from Common tools (if not Castings) some sets were to be they Port no uniform vis Guns & Stores) — I saw Sunday when I arrived in New York, and Stayed but a few hours and Could not enquire about others, but have since wrote to Col<sup>o</sup> Plaine about it & think there will be no difficulty in that point. — Hors, bobs can be got free of for the Army.

The matter now rests with you whether I hall make the Contract or not, if you should find yet to hand it to another it is too late to be lost — wish you Please to prevent the time, & terms of payments, as there must be a written agreement for the faithfull performance of the work — and they will as implicitly affect as Certain time of Payments, for which I shall look to you —

I shall not depend solely upon Col Plaine for the Castings — also intend to go and see that Damages have don at Catham, and will make haste after some in the Country — perhaps they will cost Cheaper than those in Newford — you Said something about getting Shot, howeve, not sure not mention it in your letter — you'll please to inform me wether it Shall be sold or brought to this Places, in your regt —

I do not recollect that any Compensation with your wether my Pay Could be made up for the deficiency of rank with the rest of your department, when the Office of fire armer is Opened — if you Can, and will do it — you will do me a piece of highest service — Inclosed you will Receive the return for the Month of March 1785 —

With respect & am Sir
Jo<sup>n</sup> Hodgdon Esq<sup>r</sup>                    your Hum<sup>ble</sup> ser<sup>t</sup>

Dear Sir — The enclosed letter to the Honourable the Secretary at war, with the proceedings of two several Boards of officers, I sent from this place the 11th of February last by post, but I have had no return, it is a matter of consequence to those two Gentlemen as their commutation entirely depends upon the determination of the Secretary at war — Will you be pleased to read the enclosed duplicate and send it to the war office or General Knox, who I hear is at the head of that department, and if possible get me an answer, previous to which please to take a copy for Mr Pierce —

37232:
Philad<sup>a</sup> 12 April 1783

[Town for]

Your favor of the 4<sup>th</sup> Inst. with the Inclosures by Cap<sup>t</sup> Heard came to hand on Friday last. Sebree you have allowed Jno: Roth only pay as a Soldier of Infantry, this I think is wrong as he acted in a Station superior in pay to a private and continued in that Station at the request of the Commanding Officer to the End of the War. further, on settlement of the Account of the 4<sup>th</sup> Reg<sup>t</sup> of Artillery there would be Volunteers entered in the Returns and with the approbation of M<sup>r</sup> Pierce I granted them Serjeants pay the same had been drawn for them in the pay rolls before. There is no Treasury Board formed and I understand M<sup>r</sup> Gornis has resign'd, therefore suppose it will be some time before they proceed to do any business. indeed I do not think it worth while to say any thing to them on the Subject — in my opinion you have a right to judge of Roth. Merit and grant him Gratuity, he is certainly deserving it — You must suffer me to differ in opinion with you in respect to the interest of Cap<sup>t</sup> Armstrongs & Doc<sup>t</sup> Irvines Certificates taking date the 1<sup>st</sup> Jan<sup>y</sup> 1783 you say if one Contract is not sufficient to discharge the Advances, consequent-ly the two must be put together and the Interest

cont—
37233:
March 12 1785

Inclosed are the Accounts of subsist. of the Officers of the late German Reg<sup>t</sup>. you will observe they are made up to the 1 Nov<sup>r</sup> 1779 from which period the subsist. due them was made up on settlement of their Accounts at the Treasury. M<sup>r</sup> Green will return to morrow, and as he goes on Horseback I have been under the Necessity of sending the papers by the Stage directed (by his Desire) to him, he has promised to look for them at Baltimore and send them on — those Officers who resigned previous to the 10 April 1780 are not yet their Subsist. ists to be retained — Pray inform me if Jack Hamilton has received the large Chest of Papers sent him and if you receiv'd the Money Advanc'd on that occasion — I leave you to strike the Balance and make the A/C Current.

Jo: Howell

Jn<sup>o</sup> White Esq<sup>r</sup>

37236:
Philadelphia 21. April 1785.

D<sup>r</sup> Sir

The unfortunate has always claimed some attention from me, more especially if the person has served his country with fidelity — This address arises from the application of one Joseph Jones belonging to the 2<sup>d</sup>. New York regiment, for the pay due him for his services while in the army, he informs me that he was legally discharged, which I suppose to be the case, as I find he is included in the accounts — and certificates have been issued to you for him — The poor fellow has been long afflicted with sickness and is now in a distressed situation, and unable to travel to New York —

I have therefore to request you will be so obliging, and send such certificates as may be in your hands for the said Jones, to me, for which you will have the prayers of the poor Creature and my thanks. his receipt shall be careful to take your name and transmit to you the first safe conveyance — Let me hear from you as soon as possible — I am with due Respect your very hum<sup>e</sup>

Jno. J. Howell.

Cap. Michael Connolly
agent for the New York line
37237:
New York April 23. 1785

Dear Sir

Just now I am favour'd with yours of a few days ago respecting Joseph Jones form aly of the second New York Reg<sup>t</sup>.

I am heartily sorry to hear your tender feelings are so much imposed on by one of the greatest villains that I ever knew in our line of the army. This Jones belonged to Cap Rawlings Company 2<sup>d</sup> Reg<sup>t</sup>. I have seen him bargain and get his wages gratuity and clothing, notwithstanding, I have given him a crown the day before, to relieve him or carry him home and in two or three days he imposed on two or three of his acquaintances, this was just after the dissolution of the army he has carried the joke so far as to go once or twice before a magistrate, and was sworn he never received nor voic'd or bargain'd away his pay — I think he has taken his oath twice to my knowledge.

I am sorry to hear that the well disposed part of the Community towards the army is so much imposed on especially from your quarter — as to our eastern brethren it is not a bit the better ——

For gods sake take no notice of the villain further, and if he has contracted any debts (he being a good hand at that) let him be sent to Jail or somewhere else to prevent his further impositions —— From your very Humble Serv<sup>t</sup>

J<sup>o</sup>. Howell                    Mich Connolly
37238:
Annapolis 23 April 1785.

Dear Sir

I am writing to you by post on the Subject of German Frauds. I have new fresh reason to be of belief, that Barnu actually signed the various discharges — I think it is extremely difficult to forge his manner of signing — and moreover I have evidence to prove that many of the men whose names are on the discharges, actually deserted to the enemy while they had possession of your City — I hope to hear from you on this Subject as soon as Convenient and am with my Compliments to your better half though unknown by me — Dear Howard your truly affectionate

Frinds [Richmond?]

P.S. White is just set off for Baltimore with an order for a deal of money due to M<sup>r</sup> Pierce — This will be delivered you by my friend Cap. James Smith late of Maryland Artillery

37243:
Richmond April 28. 1785

Dear Sir

Your letter of the 18<sup>th</sup> Instant Just come to hand. If you will read my letter over again you will find a contradiction to yours — what does the first paragraph say "have issued certificates agreable to the accompanying General and particular accounts current to — in the second as to Roths pay, he cannot claim more than that of a private of Infantry & do. to speak [undecipherable] but must it do." "How far does this carry any thing that bears the least towards a Stoppage or denial of pay? No and did you view the return of Certificates & claimes a copy of which Cap Ward is furnished with — and find Roths pay that of a private and without Gratuity, or that of a Sejeant of dragoons with the Gratuity.

I shew equal spirit of favor, and as high reflection on the propriety and Justness of giving merit its due private with me as any one — But from your misapprehension, my intention to give full Justice Subjected to a little delay, as [undecipherable] may be dangerous is very prettily Construed into — what Shall it — a backwardness to [undecipherable] in giving the hire to one who has granted help to raise our Independence and Secure our Liberty — No my reflection at the time would not — nor now will agree to its being kept totally from him — The amount of this State so far as the Settlement that comes within the notice of their auditors. Close on Saturday — Consequently no more will be the equal power of application
37244:
War Office 29<sup>th</sup> March 1785.

Sir

I do myself the honor to inform your Excuency and Congress that an Officer has arrived in this City from Post Harmar Nationed at Fort Mc Intosh to solicit some pay for the Troops under his Command.

The Corps will have been in Service upwards of nine Months before they can be relieved by the Troops orderd to be raised for three years. They have received one months pay only, but their necessities require that a further sum should be advanced them.

If they should receive a supply of money previously to this disbursement, and their Arrearages pleas upon a good footing, many of the Non Commissioned Officers and Privates would probably be induced to reinlist.
37245:
&c, also the other officers of the Invalid Regiment and Legion, that those officers of this State belonging thereto have also failed     I am with esteem and regard
                                                         Your Ob<sup>dt</sup> Servant
Jos. Howell Esquire                              Andw Buncomb

Annapolis April 29<sup>th</sup> 1785.

Sir     I received your letter last post with its enclosures as also your former letter, in which you express an opinion that no further explanation, respecting the charges against Captain Richard Wats can be given — I can not but differ from you on this head, and is Evident there were two officers of that Name one of which belonging to the Virginia line was a prisoner — I have Enclosed a receipt for Dennis Carsons certificate & wish you to forward me the whole of those remaining in your hands belonging by the Agent of Invalids & I will transmit a receipt for them —

Joseph Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>          John White
37246:
Elizabeth Town A<sup>p</sup> 30. 88

Upon making application to the treasurer for the Payment of the interest upon my notes I found that two of mine altho placed for monthly pay were not included in the list or return given to the treasurer The numbers of the note alluded to were 62.83/82.88 given the one for Two hundred fifty two Doll<sup>s</sup> &the other for six hundred &four Dollars. the first bearing interest from Jun 1. 1782 the from Jan. 1. 1783.

Should M<sup>r</sup> Pierce be in Philadelphia, he will recollect (but as he may not be there) I think it proper to acquaint you) that these notes were given to me by M<sup>r</sup> Pierce in this town on his way on Jury last from New York to Philadelphia and they were given as the ballance due me upon the Settlement of my Paymasters acc<sup>ts</sup> which he had just compleated &for which purpose he had brought blank certificates with him. As the treasurer cannot pay Interest upon these notes without being assured that the person in whose favor they was drawn belonged to the Jersey line &that they was not for commutation, I have to request that you will be so good as to write to &give him satisfaction

The bearer Capt. Elholm who was an officer in the late Count Pulaski's Legion until the reduction of that Corps will wait upon you for the purpose of settling his account agreeable to a resolution of Congress of the 13 Ult. which he will show you, and by which you will find you are authorized to settle and adjust his account, and that the resolution of the 22<sup>d</sup> January 1784 respecting General Armand's corps is made to extend to him. 37255:
Dear Sir,          Philadelphia May 3. 1785.

Knowing your disposition for agriculture I have taken the Liberty to propose you for a member of the Society lately established here for the promoting that art. [undecipherable] you with a certificate of your election, and a pamphlet containing an address of the Society, and a summary of its laws, and premiums offerd.

I wish this institution may give rise to a similar one in each of the States, by which one great object of this Society will be accomplished.

M<sup>r</sup> Bowdoin & M<sup>r</sup> Samuel Phillips Jun<sup>r</sup> were elected honorary members at the same time. My brother, D<sup>r</sup> John Warren, D<sup>r</sup> Waterhouse & D<sup>r</sup> Dexter were nominated at our meeting last evening. When you have opportunity to meet any of these gentlemen I persuade myself you will consult on the forming of such an institution in Massachusetts.

M<sup>r</sup> Samuel Powell  president of the Society
Tench Francis  treasurer
George Clymer
Tim Pickering secretary, are a committee of correspondence, and the Society's correspondents are requested to address their communications to the secretary.

I am dear Sir
with sincere esteem
your most obedient Servant
Tim Pickering

P.S. I add half a dozen copies of the address for you distribute among the ingenious lovers of Agriculture.

[Benj<sup>a</sup> Lincoln Esq<sup>r</sup>]

37260:
Dear Sir Philadelphia May 9. 85

As I shall be much engaged tomorrow morning on private business I have requested M<sup>r</sup> Potts to answer yours of the 5<sup>th</sup> Ins<sup>t</sup>. He will also send you the account of Cap<sup>t</sup>. Walton— By Cap<sup>t</sup> Milborne I have received Letters from M<sup>r</sup> Pierce, and among the rest is one for you, this letter being franked I have sent it, the postscreate in Order to save you money as I find by your last that I over ly pay a visit to the Comm<sup>rs</sup>. at Annapolis as well as at Philadelphia.

I cannot divine what M<sup>r</sup> Dunscomb wants by the Officers of Haylans regiment who received the Certificates of Depreciation &c horhafs he objects that part of that Reg. has rece: the final settlements and wishes to have those belonging to Virginia if this is what he means it cannot be complied with as Pennsylvania has settled for Depreciation for all the Officers Soldiers so finally and I have settled them regimentally & gave Certificates to the Agents of the Line, those Agents are now about settling their accounts with the State.

John White Esquire                                Jos Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>.

[Received from Auditor for the Interior 11 1803, R.P.O. 4126-54]
37261:
[No<sup>r</sup> 104]

Philadelphia Aug 9. 1785

Your favor of the 2 Ins<sup>t</sup>. was handed by Cap<sup>t</sup>. Elholm on the 7 Ins<sup>t</sup>. On referring to the Muster Rolls of the Legion commanded by the late Count Pulaskie for the Month of July 1779, I find that Cap<sup>t</sup> Elholm was then a Cornet and as those musters are the latest of that Legion in my possession, I could not think myself justifiable in granting his claim as Lieutenant. I have therefore closed his Account as a Cornet and granted him pay & from the time of entering the service to the day of his Release from Captivity as also his Commutation of five Years full pay in lieu of half pay for life. This settlement cannot affect any further claim which he may have, and if Congress should grant him the Rank of Lieutenant from the 1<sup>st</sup> of Sub<sup>r</sup> 1780 the day he claims it, the balance may be settled by the Commissr. at Charleston or on his application will be settled with pleasure by

Jos: Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>.

Hon<sup>ble</sup> Charles Pinckney
37262:
Savannah May 9. 1785

Dear Sir

Your Letters dated March 28 & April 8 came to hand yesterday, before this day I presume M<sup>r</sup> Audevert has received my orders for the amount of the last quarters salary — I shall attempt to retain in my hands sufficient to discharge the next quarter altho I have not enough at present — It was my wish to meet the Office at New York, that I might pursue the business immediately on my arrival, which must necessarily be retarded by the confusion of a remove — It is my opinion that it is expected the Office would remove with the others, that it cannot properly be conducted except where the Treasury Board sits — and that it will be necessary the whole Office leave Philadelphia, it being equally reasonable that the remaining claimants of Pennsylvania should come to New York as those of N. York & N. England have been obliged to come to Philad<sup>a</sup> — I therefore expect you have orders before this time, but if not as I shall so soon be with you, it is not now necessary that you remove previous to my arrival but only be in preparation to do it — I intend to sail in the first vessel that goes for Charleston, please to call at the Post Office and take up for me all my letters for fear they'll be forwarded this way, and please also to present my compliments to the Gentlemen of the Office.

P.S. If the State should not be in my name — I trust you to keep this letter in your hands — Yours &c Pierre [Joseph Howell?] Jun<sup>r</sup>
37264:
[№ 106]

Dear Sir

Philadelphia 10. Aug<sup>t</sup> 93.

By a Letter from M<sup>r</sup> White of the 8 Instant, I find you want some information relative to the Officer of Moylan's Reg<sup>t</sup> who received the Certificates of depreciation — I mentioned the matter to M<sup>r</sup> Nicholson Comptroller gen<sup>l</sup> of this State, he informs me that the whole of the regiment were received as part of the Quota of the State, settled by him individually for their depreciation to the 1<sup>st</sup> of August 1780, for which period their accounts were settled by me to the time of their several discharges —

This State & Maryland have been much imposed on by Numbers of the German Regiment who have received settlements in each state, and I wish for the same has happened in Virginia. In order to through light on this subject I wish you to send if possible a list of all the Officers & Soldiers of the Regiments you mention in yours, of the 28 April who have had settlements in your State, that I may compare them with the settlement made by M<sup>r</sup> Nicholson & myself — Cap<sup>t</sup> Pike the Agent of Moylan's Reg<sup>t</sup> did not make any distinction between those men of the Virginia State from any other, and the musters, being silent on that head, the Certificates were all given to Major Bowen & Cap<sup>t</sup> Beatty Agents for this line and endorsed by them as Pennsylvanians — Major Bowen is now settling his Accounts with the State —  And — Dunscomb Esq<sup>r</sup>

Jos Howell

37273:
By The United States in Congress assembled

March 15<sup>th</sup> 1785.

Resolved That three Commissioners with the same pay as is allowed to the Commissioners appointed for treating with the Northan Indians, be appointed to treat with the Cherokees and all other Indians Southward of them within the limits of the United States or who have been at war with them, for the purpose of making peace with them, receiving them into the favour and protection of the United States, and removing as far as may all causes of future contention or Quarrels.

That the Commissioners be instructed as a preliminary to require that all prisoners of whatever age or Sex among the Indians shall be delivered up — and they are further instructed to demand the Negroes and other property belonging to the Citizens of the United States which have been captured during the war.

That they be instructed to inform the Indians of the great occurrences of the late war, and the extent of Country relinquished by the late treaty of peace with great Britain.

That the Commissioners previous to their holding any treaty shall give due Notice of the time and place when it is to be held

Dear Sir

Philadelphia May 31. 85

There is some time since to M<sup>r</sup>. Dunscomb on the subject of Moylans Dragons &c. but have not had an answer, I much fear great impositions has taken place, as this state adopted the whole of that Regiment and settled with them both for depreciation and pay. and Virginia has also settled their depreciation to 1<sup>st</sup>. Jan<sup>ry</sup>. 1782. I flatter myself of hearing from M<sup>r</sup>. Dunscomb soon and to receive a return of all those who have had Settlements — whereby we may be enabled yet to check some — You must endeavour to spin out your business untill M<sup>r</sup>. Pierce's arrival which I hope will be next Sunday, I have some Certificate paper left but none signed that I can with any propriety spare, however, if he does not come in the time expected I must send you me Livres.

I shall keep a look out for M<sup>r</sup>. Pierce's baggage and inform you of its arrival, you have never yet inform'd me of the Arrival of Hamilton's papers & whether you got that small account for expences.

Jos. Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>.

John White Esq<sup>re</sup>.
37306:
[Pray give my former letter on this Subject, another reading, and write me again as soon as convenient.]

Joseph Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>     C. Richmond

Annapolis May — 1788

Dear Sir

I was so hurried I could not find time to write you by last post — the races having brought a large number of Officers to Town kept me employed in closing their accounts from sun to sun —

M<sup>r</sup> Dunscomb's meaning undoubtedly was the Agent of Maryland Dragoons who received the final Settlement certificates for that Corps — he wishes to know if the Agent will not be at Richmond in order to pay those Soldiers belonging to the State of Virginia who served in that reg<sup>t</sup> and it appears he has settled with several under that description before he was apprised their accounts had been settled with the public by the said Agent — M<sup>r</sup> Dunscomb you will note settles Individually, & therefore great care becomes necessary that he does not give pay to any Inhabitants of Virginia who have served in a Corps and belonging to that State may have had their arrears from the Continent settled in said corps — There is
37310:
Annapolis June 3. 1785

Just received your last letter. In answer can only observe 373 Comes on now and that the papers forming of Hamiltons is all right charge the same to me — am sick with an Intermittant and incapable of doing business — am quite low — but my Clerk is very industrious and the army business does not suffer — Jack is Young — and Crop work it is — My very [and only] Exertions towards furnishing me with Certificate paper — If M<sup>r</sup> Pierce does not arrive for any send on the one I wrote [next post].

Jos Howell                    John Rhate

[Dear Sir]                    Philadelphia June 3. 1785.

I Inclosed you will receive a lettera of the Monies belonging to me on settlement of the accounts of the late Line of Penn<sup>a</sup>. amounting to 57,682½ dollars which sum is carried to the Credit of the state in the books of the Paymaster. You will observe I have not Credited the monies advanced to Cap<sup>t</sup> Wiley & Jordan; this arose from their neglect in not inserting those advances in their Accounts & my supposing they would receive Commutation from which I generally deducted them. I shall however call on them
37311:
them and endeavour to have this mistake testified — M<sup>r</sup> Stofman also omitted Crediting the state for money lent him, but am informed by his Attorney that was done at the Treasury. Doct<sup>rs</sup> Bond & Johnston have not settled their accounts, therefore could not with propriety Certify the advances made them were passed to the Credit of the state.

John Nicholson Esq<sup>r</sup>                    Joseph Howell J<sup>r</sup>

At Sea off the Capes of the Chesapeake
June 3. 1785.

My Dear Sir.

You see I am thus far on my return; bound for N. York, if you want money as you say, then will not let the Papers go out of the Office without the rent being paid, you may let me know that I will be answerable for it — pray let the office be removed (if not done already) as soon as possible — we have hitherto had a fine passage.

Jos: Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>                    John Pierce

P.S. I have money enough to pay you should you advance any for me in the removal of the Papers. let me hear from you as soon as possible.                    John Pierce

Jos: Howell.

37317:
In Congress—
June 7<sup>th</sup> 1785.

Pensioners—

By the United States in Congress Assembled.

JUNE 7, 1785.

RESOLVED,

THAT it be and it is hereby recommended to the several States, to make provision for officers, soldiers or seamen, who have been disabled in the service of the United States, in the following manner, viz.

1. A complete list shall be made out by such person or persons as each State shall direct, of all the officers, soldiers or seamen resident in their respective States, who have served in the army or navy of the United States, or in the militia in the service of the United States, and have been disabled in such service, either in consequence of military duty, or of obtaining a livelihood by labour. In this list shall be expressed the pay, duty and disability of each invalid, also the regiment, corps or ship to which he belonged, and a copy of the same shall be transmitted to the office of the Secretary at war, within one year from this date, and each State shall be annually [undecipherable] the respective list of the invalids relident in the respective states; shall from year to year be annually transmitted to the office of the Secretary at war.

2. No officer, soldier or seaman, shall be considered as an invalid, or entitled to pay, unless he can produce a certificate from the commanding officer or surgeon of the regiment, ship, corps or company in which he served, or from a physician or surgeon of a military hospital or other good and sufficient testimony, setting forth his disability, and that he was thus disabled while in the service of the United States.

3. That all commissioned officers within the aforesaid description, disabled in the service of the United States, so as to be wholly incapable of military duty or of obtaining a livelihood, be allowed a yearly pension equal to half of their pay respectively. And all commissioned officers as aforesaid, who shall not have been disabled to so great a degree, be allowed a yearly pension which shall correspond with the degree of their disability compared with that of an officer wholly disabled: That all non-commissioned officers and privates within the aforesaid description, disabled in the service of the United States so as to be wholly incapable of military or garrison duty, or of obtaining a livelihood by labour, be allowed a sum not exceeding five dollars per month: And all non-commissioned officers and privates as aforesaid, who shall not have been disabled to so great a degree, be allowed a sum which shall correspond with the degree of their disability, compared with that of a non-commissioned officer or private wholly disabled.

4. That each State appoint one or more persons of suitable abilities, to examine all claimants, and to report whether the person producing a certificate, setting forth that he is an invalid, be such in fact, and if such, to what pay he is entitled; and thereupon, the person authorized to make such enquiry, shall give to the Invalid a certificate specifying to what pay he is entitled, and transmit a copy to the person who may be appointed by the State to receive and record the same.

5. That each State be authorized to pay to the commissioned officers, non-commissioned officers and privates, the sum or sums to which they shall be respectively entitled: Provided that the [undecipherable] aforesaid pensions and other allowances, to be deducted from the respective quotas of the States for the year on which they shall be made. Provided that no officer who has accepted or given commutation for half pay, shall be entered on the list of invalids, unless he shall have first returned his commutation.

West Point 3 June 1785

S<sup>ir</sup>

I received yours of the 3<sup>d</sup> Instant by M<sup>r</sup> Mauow, also Eleven Men Come with him — and [strikethrough: brought] me a receipt for the Money they had received. — You will please to send me a Copy of M<sup>c</sup>Con tracking agreements, as I believe some of them will not perform the Proffer — and if you have not agreed to hire them for a Certain Time, I should think it best to Discharge them — but, should that be the Case, we must make the Most of them. — Should you engage any more you must not Promise buildings for their Families, as they Cannot be Supplied —

There is no Suitable place at Prospect for the Arms, but the best thing is to Come here when he returns from the eastward and We'll determine what Shall [strikethrough: best] be Done with them — [strikethrough: Inclosed] Inclos'd you will receive an Abstract for what Service has been Done In the Department — if Possible pray find the Money by the Bearer M<sup>c</sup>Lattie, who puts on to M<sup>r</sup> Francs, as all the Men are Soldiers And will be Discharg'd in a few days, and are Continually Dunning me, I Should have Sent it before, had you not Promised to Come here in my last. I write you about my own Accounts and Should be glad to have upon the [undecipherable] Subject — I am S<sup>ir</sup> Your Most Humble Serv<sup>t</sup>

William Price

37336:
A  PROCLAMATION.

**W**HEREAS, it hath been represented to the United States in Congress assembled, That several disorderly Persons have crossed the *River Ohio*, and settled upon their unappropriated Lands :— *And whereas*, it is their Intention, as soon as it shall be surveyed, to open Offices for the Sale of a considerable Part thereof, in such Proportions, and under such other Regulations as may suit the Convenience of all the Citizens of the said States, and others, who may wish to become Purchasers of the same :— and as such Conduct tends to defeat the Object which they have in view, is in direct Opposition to the Ordinances and Resolutions of Congress, and highly disrespectful to the Federal Authority —— they have therefore thought fit, and do hereby issue this their Proclamation, strictly forbidding all such unwarrantable Intrusions; and enjoining all those who have settled thereon to depart, with their Families and Effects, without Loss of Time, as they shall answer the same at their Peril.

DONE by the United States in Congress assembled, the fifteenth Day of June, One Thousand Seven Hundred and Eighty-five, and the Ninth Year of the Independence of the United States of America.

*Richard Henry Lee*, P.

*Charles Thomson*, Sec'ry.

*N E W - Y O R K:*

Printed by F. Childs, No. 17, Duke-Street.

Dear Sir

March last with its enclosures relative to the honorary presents of swords and medals to His Excellency General Washington and other gallant Officers I take the earliest opportunity of communicating with you upon the subject.

37369:
Fort M<sup>c</sup>Intosh, July 16 1785.

Sir, on the 11<sup>th</sup> instant, I am informed that three chiefs, (and some from Indians) of the Six Nations had arrived at Fort Pitt, and wished very much to speak to the Commanding Officer.

Agreeably to this desire, I rode up to Pittsburg and met them in Council, when to my surprise the Complaynte, the principal chief, had the original articles of the treaty which was signed last October at Fort Stanwix, along with him, and towards the close of the speech, said they were burthesome, and wished to deliver them up. I have been pleased to inclose to you their speech, and my answer to it.

It is reported that a Mr Brant has Lately raised from London who (will the commanding officer of the British in that quarter.) has informed the Six Nations that their Country has been ceded to the Congress by the King of Great Brittain. In consequence of which they charge Congress of base, dishonest by this act of treating, and say they are in danger of their own people.

We have left Fort Pitt fully satisfied by to appearances with the temper of their chiefs; but the Complaints in their possession of the British, it is very evident that all treaties held by us with the Indians, will have but little weight with them.

Your most Obedient Servant
Jos. Harmar

The Honorable Major General Knox, secretary at War, New York.

[Pay Office]

Pay Office New York Aug 19<sup>th</sup> 1785 —

Agreably to your request of the 9<sup>th</sup> instant I examined the Files of the late Auditor of the Army, but do not find that Lieut William Thompson made any settlement with them, wherein the State of Pennsylvania is credited with monies advanced him for the recruiting or any other service —

I find that M<sup>r</sup> Thompson made a settlement with M<sup>r</sup> Johnston Auditor of accounts on the 17 Jan<sup>y</sup> 1783. This appears to be the only one on record except such as has been for his pay or expence on command — M<sup>r</sup> Johnstons record is as follows — "Lieut William S Thompson of the 9<sup>th</sup> Pennsylvania Reg<sup>t</sup> being charged with the sum of Fifty nine Dollars received of M<sup>r</sup> Coulton P.M<sup>r</sup> to the Board of War, which sum being paid, the Balance is receipt produced, the same is discharged"

The Auditors were not authorized to admit any extra charge for bounty or premiums that was advanced by any State, consequently they could not admit any credit being given by the State for monies advanced him for the recruiting service by the State — I am sensible there are very few of any instances where the Auditors have taken notice of the monies advanced by the different States to their Officers —

Please to direct to me, under cover to John Bray Esq<sup>r</sup>, this saves postage, or at least it will spare me the trouble of opening an act with the States for that purpose —

The Col wishes [undecipherable] to yours & I am as ever
[undecipherable]                             Your Ob<sup>t</sup> Hum<sup>bl</sup> S<sup>t</sup>

Sir

I had a Letter from Major Allen M<sup>c</sup>Lane, Col<sup>l</sup> of his Legion, in answer to one I wrote him on the Subject of an advance by the State of Maryland, in which he requests the same may be charged to the United States, to be accounted for by him on final Settlement, — and although I have already transmitted the Account to your Office, I have thought proper to mention the Matter again, lest that might have been mislaid. — The Sum charged to Major M<sup>c</sup>Lane is £187.10.0 New Currency paid in March 1781, at the Rate of [undecipherable] for 1. = £57.13.10 Specie, for which Sum please to charge him, and give the State of Maryland credit therefor. — The Certificates for Horses taken and purchased by Major M<sup>c</sup>Lane within this State, have all been taken up and liquidated by the State, and will be charged to the United States in Gen<sup>l</sup> Account. — In answer to your Letter of the 13<sup>th</sup> July, on the subject of the advance to Baron de Kalb — I have to inform you that I never received any Money from him, nor did I ever hear of his having taken up any Money for the purposes you mention, until I received your Letter. — As soon as I receive answers to the Enquiries I have set on foot touching this Business, I will write to you again thereon. With high Regard I am Sir Your obed<sup>t</sup> hble Serv<sup>t</sup>

New York Aug 4<sup>th</sup> 1785
Sir,

My indisposition has prevented my answering your Letter of the 10 ins't sooner - It is the wish of Mr Pierce to close the accounts of the Army as soon as possible and which I expect we shall be able to accomplish some time next Spring - It will therefore be best for you to proceed to this City as soon as your convenience will admit - You will be carefull to bring all the papers and Vouchers necessary to complete your business - If you should find it necessary to write to me previous to your arrival, direct to me under cover to Mr Pierce as all Letters to him are free -

Your H<sup>ble</sup> Serv
Joseph Howell Jr.

M<sup>r</sup> John Weston

37415:
Philadelphia August 27, 1785.

My dear General,

Major Dunn writes to me from Providence that he had seen you at Boston, and that he left you well — I need not repeat to you the pleasure, which every relation of your happiness conveys to me — impressed with sentiments of affection and esteem, which no length of time, or change of circumstance can lessen, I shall ever continue to cherish the remembrance of your friendship with pride and satisfaction, and to regard your goodness with encrease of gratitude.

I am now to communicate to you, my respected friend, an alteration of intention relating to myself which will [undecipherable] your concurrence and approbation.

The general embarrassment of commerce, which, at this moment affects even the merchant of established credit and extensive capital, have determined me, with other considerations, to retire from business while I can do so with advantage —

Unwilling to [undecipherable] a life of speculation under such precarious circumstances as now govern the trade of this country — and Still [more] disposed to involve others who, with

views of friendship towards me, might share in any unfortunate event. I have yielded to the [undecipherable] requests of all my friends here, and to propensities of my own, which have likewise compelled me — and I have resolved to add me more to the already redundant list of lawyers.

Among those Gentlemen, who have pressed me to this pursuit are President Dickerson, Doctor Rush, General St. Clair, and Mr Wilton, who becomes my Instructor — "all of them assure me that a dispensation of the term prescribed for studying the law will readily be obtained in my favor, whenever I am found qualified — and Mr Wilton does not hesitate to pronounce that eighteen months close application will bestow the requisites of a Counsellor —— Perhaps I am flattered by delusive self love, when I imagine my literary attainments are not inferior to a [undecipherable: majority] of our lawyers — and I may be equally mistaken in the estimation of my mental powers when I presume to rate them by a similar standard — but whatever my aptitude, or my retentive faculties may be — I dare venture to vouch, with confidence for those acquirements, which an effectual application will confer.

Arrived at an age when the levities of youth have lost their

influence, and the conscious necessity of unremitted attention has established itself. my friends will have nothing to apprehend for me on that score —— You, my dear General, are perhaps better empowered than any other person to decide upon the probability of my success — you have had an opportunity of observing my inclinations, and of tracing my talents from their dawn (if they may be said to have dawn) — The one, I trust, you have at no time found vicious — and the other I hope you have held to be susceptible of science —— Such opinions as these have outweighed the opposite suggestion of taking up a profession at twenty seven — and the probable mortifications to which so late an hour of Study may be obnoxious.

I have reasoned in this manner —

My health is, or appears to be, well established — This will capacitate close application — and close application, unless self love has greatly abused me, must [undecipherable] an equal quantum of knowledge with many of my competitors — perhaps enable me to outstrip some of them — This procures practice — practice produces wealth — wealth creates consequence — and that last of consequence too, as being the combined result of property and knowledge, which no other vehicle in society appears to promise —— These are conclusions, which I cannot consider as either unnatural or improbable — and supposing them to be

completed within Ten years — I shall certainly have attained a superior station in the community than any, even the most successful, line of commerce could have led to in the same time.

I could not enter upon so entire a change of object without communicating my intentions to you — I shall be happy to learn that your opinion gives them an additional sanction — for in whatever situation the events of life may surprise me — I shall ever esteem it amongst the most grateful of my privileges to subscribe myself with all the reality of truth and affection, my dear General,

Your sincerely obedient Servant

W. [undecipherable]

[Philadelphia
Aug 27<sup>th</sup> 1785]

New York October 4<sup>th</sup> 1785

Sir.

Your letter to M<sup>r</sup> Pierce of the 3<sup>d</sup> September 1785 came to hand this day— as M<sup>r</sup> Pierce is absent I have to inform you that I have made application to the Comptroller Generals Office, and find that no such certificate as you described in your letter has yet been presented to that Office to be cancelled or examined—

But as there were five certificates issued by M<sup>r</sup> Pierce to the 1<sup>st</sup> Barnes for the same sum (Viz. 1000 dollars each) it will be necessary that you ascertain the number of that which you have lost, that we may be able to distinguish it— You will please to direct under Cover to M<sup>r</sup> Pierce.

I am Sir with due respect
Your very H<sup>ble</sup> Ser.
Joseph Howell Jun. Afft.

Joseph Ward Esquire

37481:
War <sup>r</sup> Office 8 Oct<sup>r</sup> 1785

Dear Sir

To many evils which are daily experienced by the Officers of the late Massachuts, falls due in the Continental army, from a want of the money due to them for services and is now [strikethrough: or] ought to be in [strikethrough: the] hands of the collectors calls aloud for a representation of their Sufferings to the General court, which meets on the 19<sup>th</sup> instant, and pray that some effectual measures may be adopted to bring forward a speedy collection of the taxes and punctual payments, by the collectors, into the Treasury office. And to pray also that if provision is not made for to redeem the Notes due in 84. & 85 it may be immediately done. — I have lately been flattered from a quarter I have great confidence in that, but it an application being made have the best consequences — I want it must be attention of the General court and proper expectations formed thereon it will instantly stamps the notes with a new value. —

I am fully in opinion that the Officers of the massachuts falls should come together as soon as possible. I wish therefore you would confort G<sup>o</sup> Health as it is not a lax tax meeting I write, doubtably agreeable with us I urge you to write both Brooks and y<sup>r</sup> Officer in town on this point, if they, or you without consulting

them, may notify one to be held in Boston the latter end of this month or y<sup>e</sup> beginning of Nov<sup>r</sup> request the Journal news writers to insert it in their several papers —

I have the honor of being
D<sup>r</sup> Sir your obedient
Servant & affectionate
friend
B Lincoln

Gen Jackson —

[B. Gen<sup>l</sup> Jackson
Oct 8, 1785]

37487:
Tuesday October 11. 1785

[VII]<sup>th</sup> is assembled — Present as yesterday —

A report from the board of treasury, to which was referred a petition of Lieut. Nichols and Richard Boyd agents to the invalide corps and Harens regiments, in behalf of themselves with age nts of Armands Corps, the agent at artife. in and the corps of Sappers and miners.

Resolved That when the agents appointed to the certificate orders, or the balances due to the corps of the late continental army (nor appertaining to any state) shall have finally adjusted the accounts of their respective corps with the commissioners of army accounts the board of treasury be, and it is hereby authorised, to ascertain and report to Congress, such compensation as from the report of the said Commissioners, they shall judge intimated to the justice of the respective —

Resolved. That the certificates which are or delivered to the respective agents above mentioned, to the continental troops, to whom they belong be returned to the commissioners of army accounts, with whom the said agents are to settle the accounts of their agents, relative to the balances due to the individuals aforesaid, and the commissioners for army accounts shall transmit, such remaining certificates to the Supreme executive authority of the State, in which the parties having a right to receive the same, are enrolled in the same manner as is provided for in the resolves of Congress of the 3<sup>d</sup> of November 1785 and N<sup>o</sup> of May 1785 in the case of regimentals appertaining to the several States.

37490:
[Fort Post, 21 Let<sup>r</sup> 1785 —]                                                                 15

I have sent by M<sup>c</sup> Connelly the Pay abstract for the Detafsion employ'd at this place for the months of August past unto Oct<sup>r</sup> 1785 — of Confinement I should be glad to have the Money first part as the men immediately upon their Disfemission, as in the Course of the months they have Contracted some Debts for the Subsistance of their Families which they expect to pay before they leave this place —

On the 28<sup>th</sup> of this month there will be but one more of Cap<sup>t</sup> John flints Company on duty by reason of their times expiring — was to be informed who I must apply for for men to work and Guard, the Garrison after that period.

This is four Wood was left by Col Pickering for the use of the Garrison, and there is no forage at Deposit for them, please to let me know within the whole or part Shall be kept and if kept, in what manner they Shall be Supplied, and the quantity for daily allowance.

In the estimate made for wood when you was here there was no allowance made for the Garison, that meet the Right of Confederation Blends, Fort Clinton & Putnam. Nor any allowance made for Negro Servants the winter inferring, neither the Officers & men that is like to be here after the last of this month. Exclusive of the one Officer my Self and Thirty five men which the estimate was made for — please to inform me how far the deficiences must be Supplied.

There is a hundred and several of Boggon Covers here which if they are not disposed for Some other Partition will make good Beds to Cover the men, as it is and within too Short [strikethrough: dock] to make large Boats — I received three Bales of Knapsacks and a fumar box of Medicines by M<sup>c</sup>Sean I am for &c

William Rice
D<sup>r</sup> [undecipherable] Atkins

War Depart 26<sup>th</sup> Oct<sup>r</sup> 1781 —

Sir, I have Considered the Refitting and Altering the Barrels in the Garrison. Now when returned to the Barrels I take Seventy One and a half short in No good Condon — but the Damages agreeable to the within Before this: But as the Damage at Seven costs forty one which makes in the whole Seventy two and half more than was estimated — then was three hundred and fifty Barrels taken out was then has first Returned, which it took to fill the Barrels agreeable to the Report Returns as every Barrel taken not fell Short of being over Very Considerable from one eighth to one fourth, and four to one half —

Enclosed is the Returns of the Ordnance Military I wrote to months there for the month of October 1781 — there was two hundred and forty seven Repaired and Cleaned during the month, which makes in the whole Eighteen hundred and Sixty four which has been done during the paper — agreeable to your Order the Armounr & Coopry is Discharged the day above mentioned —

I am &c
The Honorable                                    William Price

37503:
New-ark October 25<sup>th</sup> 1785

Dear Sir

Your letters respecting the propositions you had before [strikethrough: you] assigned to the Surgeon accounts have been considered by me.

It may be necessary first to offer an Apology to you for interfering in this business, which was occasioned by the repeated remarks made on your letters at the Secretary and by the Members of Congress and in particular by an observation of one of the latter, in the House, that not a full thing [alluding to [undecipherable: continuing] the subject he was speaking of] would be clear even to the clouded mind of M<sup>r</sup> Doniscent.

On reviewing your letters I find two faults in their composition, the first is that you use words sometimes in a different sense than any other person and the second is that you affect a style of play and writing — whereas you would write with enough if your sense was conveyed in the most simple & common language, unadorned with any ornament and as near conversation as possible — all letters ought to be clear and plain and take our common discourse except a little more correct — I delayed writing until this time in hopes the grand Committee before whom yours and all the Commissioners doubts and difficulties are laid, had reported to Congress.

I have Considered the Refitting and Altering the Provision to the Garrison Arms when returned to Fright Barrels. New York - Nov 1, 1793

Sir

I have received your account of the Negotiation. I know so much that every part of your report is justifiable, if any were to blame he is justified to have the evidence. That I may be on the whole have not appear clear, and recurring to the War Office and the Treasury Board. I design to make out a second account on each of the different class of affairs — the first, those that were charged to continue and in consequence thereof remaining accounts in the service to the end of the war — the second those that were charged and when pay they were raised — the third those that were charged and were paid afterwards for reasons of record against their account — I [undecipherable] from the same that were arranged, but not entitled to pay on other terms than are now the rules you will think give us the last [?] class of the different claims of the officers —

You mention your last return, made in October of the regimental staff, from which I suppose there is meant to be paid — in your first report there were a number of officers made up for pay, whose names were omitted in your last return, for [undecipherable] reasons (on No. 3) I wish to know if this is intentional and whether they are to be included, and on the contrary where mention of officers not noted in the first report, which I suppose arises from information received from the Army. There are a class of officers I cannot determine, being arranged or discharged on an abset for in rights they were given either from wounds, from suffering, or being absent, as I understand my friends, to compleat that of those found —

37520:
War Office 7 Nov. 1785.

Sir,

Congress have thought proper, in consideration of the impracticability of marching troops to the frontiers during the winter months, to postpone the further recruiting of those until the ensuing March. I have written to Major [undecipherable] to forward all the recruits he may have engaged to West Point and if he should still be deficient twenty five men or upwards, that he attend personally to the orders of Congress, and send the recruiting of them until the time aforementioned; but that if he should want only a few men to complete the number of his establishment, he should direct that they be recruited immediately and forwarded to West Point. I have thought it consistent with my duty to inform your Excellency of this circumstance.

I have the honor to be with the highest respect and esteem,

Your Excellency's
most obedient Servant

H Knox

His Excellency,
Governor Griswold.

November 12. 1783

Sir,

It appearing necessary that a provied should be fixed for the final adjustment of all claims against the United States for services performed by the Army, and that from the nature of such claims they cannot be as properly investigated by any other Officer as the present Commissioner who has nearly closed the limited settlement, The United States in Congress assembled on the 2<sup>d</sup> instant have been pleased to pass the inclosed Resolution limiting the time for exhibiting such claims to the first of August next and have directed the Commissioner of army accounts to give public notice thereof in all the Notes for the space of six months. —

With great Respect

Your Excellency's

Dear Sir, I yesterday received your favor of the 23 — I enclose Mr White's letter that it may be answered — I wrote you some days since informing of my arrival — since which I have scarcely been out of my Chamber, having all the affairs of my fathers estate to arrange, and to put into posture for settlement — I desired you to wait on the Commissioners of the Treasury, do be informed if any thing occurs which will make my presence necessary; You will yourself know if I am wanted in the office; and wish to be informed when it will be necessary for me to return — as I shall stay here as long as I can — or until the beginning of January — please to present my Compliments to the Gentlemen of the office.

War Office 2<sup>d</sup> December 1785

Dear Sir,

I have to acknowledge the receipt of your favor of the 21<sup>st</sup> ultimo.

The board of treasury at my instance will remit you immediately an order for the sum of four hundred dollars on their Agent in Maryland.

I will thank you to inform me of the receipt of the sum that I may have the pleasure of ending you an official letter on the occasion.

I expect in the course of a month or two to receive all the swords which were voted by Congress as testimonies of their special approbation, upon receiving them I shall have the pleasure of transmitting yours.

I am,
Dear Sir,
With great sincerity
Your affectionate
Humble servant

H Knox

Col<sup>o</sup>
Tench Tilghman

I received yours of 5<sup>th</sup> of Nov<sup>r</sup>, also the Clothing agreeable to the Invoice, — Those sent by M<sup>r</sup> Isaac Garrison are handed, and Sytten Waggon Covers agreeable to your Letter —

I am Sir &c

William Ville
Dep<sup>y</sup> Com — Affairs

The Honorable Major Gen<sup>l</sup> Knox
Secretary at War —

Dear Sir,

New York January 16. 1786

Your favor of the 6<sup>th</sup> Inst reached me on thursday last, and would have been answered by the first of Yesterday if I could have completed the enquiries necessary to grant a Certificate for David Murray, which was done and enclosed — there is a David Murray of Grubbs Co inlisted the 29 April 1777, but I suppose deserted in August following as he is not on the rolls of that or any succeeding Month.

M<sup>r</sup> Pierce arrived on the 4<sup>th</sup> Inst. and has been very busy in Col<sup>o</sup> Nicols affair — he expects to compleat the business today, so that I expect you will receive a packet from him, by the same post that conveys this — as soon as Leisure will permit, I will take up the act<sup>t</sup> of Cap<sup>t</sup> Thompson.

Cap. M. M. Sennell

37589:
[1786, March 14
*illegible*]

S I R,                                                    War-Office, April 25, 1785.

On the memorial of captain Jesse Grant and captain Jesse Cook, referred to me from Congress, I beg leave to report,—

That captain Jesse Grant and captain Jesse Cook, late officers in the Connecticut line, were taken prisoners at Fort-Washington in November, 1776, and detained whilst in captivity, to the beginning of the year 1779.— That as officers retiring from Service under the resolution of Congress of the 3<sup>d</sup> of October, 1778, they are entitled to one years pay as a gratuity, and to a full Settlement of their pay and Subsistence to the time they were exchanged, but to no other allowance, either for half pay or depreciation of pay.

Should Congress however be of opinion, that the peculiar Situation of those officers, added to their Services, entitles them to more, entire to the favourable notice of the honorable House, they will be pleased to relieve,—

It is recommended to the State of Connecticut, to Settle with Jesse Grant and Jesse Cook, late officers in the Service of the United States, the depreciation of pay according to their respective ranks from the time they entered into Service, to the 5th day of October, 1780, when they returned from captivity.

I have the honor to be, with the highest respect,
Your Excellency's most obedient humble Servant,
H. Knox.

His Excellency,
The President of Congress.

---

The Committee consisting of M<sup>r</sup>. Dane, M<sup>r</sup>. Lee and M<sup>r</sup>. St. Clair, to whom was referred the representation of J. Pierce Esq. commissioner of army accounts, of the 2d of March, 1786, Stating that several officers of the continental line of the army who were made prisoners in the years 1776 and 1780, and continued in captivity until the exchanges were made after the month of October 1780, found themselves, on their return, out of Service from the changes made during their captivity differently organized, and their names omitted in the list of officers, and the Said officers claim the benefit of the resolutions of the relieves of October 2d and 11th, 1780, thereby granted to distressed and certain other officers—Report,

That they have carefully examined, considered and compared the Several resolves of Congress relative to this Subject of January 19, May 15, and November 26, 1778; May 22, 1779; October 2 and 11, and November 28, 1780; January 1, 1781; and November 13, 1781; January 26, and February 20, 1782; and March 8, 1783; and are of opinion, on a true construction of the last resolve, that the benefits and emoluments of their resolves of October 2 and 11, 1780, cannot be extended to officers in captivity, excluded, their names omitted in the list of officers, and their troops differently organized as aforesaid, for it appears to have been the uniform intention of Congress to extend these benefits and emoluments to those descriptions only of officers in the line, within which of which are the officers referred to, by the commissioner; to wit, to officers who should continue in Service during the war; to officers who Should be Subjects to, and liable to be called for by the enemy, and to officers distressed by the two changes in the army, made in the years 1780 and 1781. That the aforesaid officers are therefore entitled to one years pay, granted by the resolve of November 24, 1778, and extended by the resolve of May 26, 1779.

37591:
Hartford December 12<sup>th</sup> 1788.

Dear Sir,

While on a journey to Connecticut on a visit to my friends I yesterday received your letter acquainting me that you had seen a resolution of Congress admitting the time of bringing in Claims into my Office, from which you suppose that it may be reasonable to extend your time for receiving the Claims of the Line of Virginia — this resolution is founded on a supposition that all the Officers had closed in the States and that the remaining claims are only to be admitted in my office — I cannot suppose that a further continuance of your Office will be found, but should any claim come in while you are making out the stating of your accounts — I presume there has been to their adjustment will not only be an act of friendships and generosity will be acceded to by the most [undecipherable]

I wish you however to make out and forward your accounts as early as possible — my principal reason to be urgent is to finish the business of my Office that I may be ready to have it by the first of August, and to be prepared of your registries that the forwarding may not be retarded —

I suppose you have received my letter inclosing the Names of such Officers as I wanted further Information respecting their Pay — I have not presented this return for want of the necessary information and have no doubt but the more
37592:
and when I joined the company at Walleys Ranges in a pewdery Purse appointed 1797 to the regiment; and was sent to denning-town with a parcel of Horses to recruit — My Bounties from the Regiment is fourteen months twenty six days at 10 Dollars ℗ Month — then joined Col: Mitchell D Martin, and continues with him, the Col: M<sup>r</sup> Molen took his place — he then asked for my papers with a promise to pay me the next day, and that night left the town, and I never saw him since — all this time I never received one farthing from him, but lives on my own money for Two Three thousand Dollars on their Expenced — I spoke to Mr Benson, one of the orders, on the subject, he says M<sup>r</sup> Molen never settled with him; I told his discontent with him too — this is the way I have been served — I pray Heav. will be so kind to assist me in the recovery of my just due, so put me in a way to obtain it — Whatever manner

Your sincere Friend
James Bragg

Jos Howell Esq<sup>r</sup>

Philadelphia December 10 1796

Sir

I am favored with yours of the 3<sup>d</sup> Inst with the certificate made agreeable to my request, for which you will please to accept my best thanks — I enclose M<sup>r</sup> gallins Sum from the Board of War, which you will please to present to him with my best respects — I shall take it as a particular favor if you'd ask General Knox if he has made any report on my account as to foreign, and to let me know whether or not — Your compliance will greatly oblige

Your And Hble Ser<sup>t</sup>
James McLean

Jos Howell Esq<sup>r</sup>
37593:
[152]

New York Dec. 19, 1785 —

Dear Sir,

Your favor of the 2<sup>d</sup> Instant, with the register, were duly received — the accompanying it the payments made by Henry Caldwell, & Lieut Col. Richardson, were December 2, 1776, paid pay for the Supernumeraries of the regiment to the last day of November 1776, which have reason to suppose the regiment was discharged at the last day of June & 1776, limits the power of his Superiors, in the last day that says — This brought me out of Capt Johnson, have another claim — it further appears that Cap. Jenkins received on the 2<sup>d</sup> December following yours, from Gentleman the sum of £174 pistoles, & bought of his Company — Major Lloyd not yet settled his accounts, some time since I have enquired in this particular relating to John Hoops, amounting to £4 7<sup>7</sup>6 8 left, to be forwarded to Mr. Richmond in consequence of some charges which the State had a general lien, a settlement not covered to his debt on settlement of the regimental accounts — you mention a Cap. James Hughes, this was more of that name in the regiment who had a commission — you will please to inform yourself of that fact, and the person meant, if so, invite and you the Certificate.

As the frontier goes very fast with the registers, I must beg you to forward by every post, at least three times the Number you send last, as any delay on the part of the Office would be very agreable to the Public, who I have reason to believe is sick of the contract — I am informed he has already entered a complaint to the Board of Treasury of the inattention paid to the papers brought to the Office — this complaint however is not yet announced officially.

As Mr. Pierce is absent on a visit to his friends, and not expected for some time, it would make me much happy were any thing to arise to his Prejudice or my dishonor

John White Esq. Jo: Howell
37594:
[154]

New York Dec. 16 1785

Dear Sir,

Your favors of the 9<sup>th</sup> & 14 Instant were received this day — on the former you inform me of writing me on the 5<sup>th</sup> Ult<sup>o</sup> — that letter is not come to hand.

I shall be particularly careful how I submit the charges of Capt Oliver, without proper vouchers being produced — at present there is a rumor but he informs me that they are in possession of General Floyd, or at least, that he gave them to him some years ago.

Charlotte to your letter was forwarded by M<sup>r</sup> Moore — the answer will be produced by him also.

Enclosed is a letter from M<sup>r</sup> White of a private nature about I presume — M<sup>r</sup> Dummovit has transmitted the returns of those Officers names which were wanted — and on this day I received a letter from him making trifling charges against Col. Villers Willis, in this letter he says — you have with my reasons I have it completed as well as my Books & plan last settlement — I know of no consequence to run the record by me, nor do I think it right for it to pass of any hand, until my account is settled and a full discharge given me, as I should any accident happen I may be much injured — from the above I have reason to fear we could not have here again in time the consequence will be a stoppage of the Repositories before I could be very agreeable to M<sup>r</sup> White who of me an much assistance to both of the posts.

I have wrote M<sup>r</sup> Dummovit begging he to send his register informing him if they do not arrive in thirty days from this, that the Posts must Stop and if this is applied the blank were immediately rest with us — the Diary is in capacity, they now reads of the Diary.

Joseph Howell

John Pierce Esq<sup>r</sup>.
37595:
New York December 14, 1785

Dear Sir

Your letter to M<sup>r</sup> Dean relative to giving your office I have forwarded to him in Connecticut where it lies with the care assist to his friends — The printers go on rapidly with the register, and I much fear that the work will be delayed for want of your alterations earnestly entreat you to forward them on immediately, either by post, or such other conveyance as you may think proper — as a calculation I find that your register will be wanted in twenty three days from this — the time is short, and the necessity of having them at the expiration of the time is great. Believe therefore, you will exert your good talents on this occasion

J. Trennond Esq<sup>r</sup>

Jos Hewitt

Dear Sir Philadelphia Dec 15. 1785

Just on the eve to leave this city, I have only time to acknowledge the receipt of your last favor, and to inform you that as the time will not come in upon a foot, there will in full time to attend the meeting. I have written relative hereto, and am to trouble M<sup>r</sup> Prince with a return, completed to be granted, and the Papers to a Lawyer. M<sup>r</sup> Connell who will repay, on my account any expence attending this business — Your attentions professed to my care, are I assure you, most refreshing, and had sufficient proof thereof, a grateful sense of which will always be retained by

Lewis Nicola

Joseph Hewitt Esq<sup>r</sup>
37597:
[156]

[Pay Office]

Dear Sir:                    New York, Dec 15, 1785

In answer to your letter of the 11<sup>th</sup> Instant, I have to inform you that the pay Accounts of Col: Pickering are not closed in this Office and that you have rendered the on rent — appointing the Issuing Commissaries for the department would have done it. Sooner the application, but am informed that he is not authorised to do him — Mr Downing's clearly of passage, that you can perhaps comes under the State of Mr Pick as one fourth of the debt is to pay, and becomes due Samnion 1<sup>st</sup> January 1790.

Mr Downing has exhibited a charge a gamma sum of $388.75 Lawful money on Clothing advanced your regiment between the months of April & October 1780 — can you give my information within on our Accounts from the pay of the regiment & if it has been accounted for — I beg you answer as soon as possible as the Books Stand open and while so the charge must appear against you.

The three Months pay drawn for you by Ebam Chambers was creditor by him on account remained from in Dec 1780. there appeared a larger amount due from Col Chambers which distance had not been paid into the Treasury of the United States consequently the account is by not closed which would prevent me from making you a certificate for the pay of three months — we have been thought it a private transaction, and which & loudly admits the discharge to come, knowing if the balance had been paid at the time the amount was remmitted, you would have only reserved the nomimal sum, about five dollars Grace — This indeed me on his regimental account to return from him the sum of five hundred dollars which sum is still in the hands of the public — if you could settle this matter with Colonel Chambers, I think it would be to your advantage.

Joseph Howell

Gen<sup>t</sup> Edward Hand

In answer to your letter of the 11<sup>th</sup> Instant, I have to inform you that the certificates of Col. Pickering are not cancelled in this Office, and that you have reminded the one sent — appointing the Running Commissioners for this department would have done it. As to the application, I am informed that he is not authorised to do it — Mr. Dearing's is clearly of promise, that you can be comes under the state of all Public, as the head of the debt is so pay, and becomes due January 1<sup>st</sup> January 1790.

Mr Dearing has exhibited a charge in a gross sum of [undecipherable: $133.75] Lawful money for Clothing delivered your regiment between the months of April & October 1780 — can you give any information whether the same was deducted from the pay of the regiment, if it has been accounted for — as I beg you answer as soon as possible as the Books stand open, and while so the charge must appear against you.

The three Months pay drawn for you by [Samuel] Chambers was credited by him, in our account examined given in Dec<sup>r</sup> 180, there appeared a large remainder from Col. Chamber's which balance has not been paid into the Treasury of the United States; consequently the account is not closed, which must prevent me from making out a certificate for the pay of three months — in the mean time this is a private transaction, and which hardly admits the existing; be sure, knowing if the balance had been paid at the time the amount was examined, you would have only received the nominal sum, about five dollars grace. This induced me on this top the regimental account, to obtain from him the sum of five hundred dollars which sum is still in the hands of the public — if you could settle this matter with [Samuel] Chambers, I think it would be to your advantage.

Joseph Howell

Gen<sup>l</sup> Edward Hand
37601:

Jos. Howell Esq<sup>r</sup>

Luckfield Dec 21<sup>st</sup> 95

Dear Sir,

Your favor of the 15<sup>th</sup> came to hand this evening — I have written a letter to Mr Tennant which I inclose to be sent on forth a copy may be taken previous to your forwarding it — After you receive this letter you will not have an opportunity to write me and be, as I shall be on my return before it can reach me — I intend to set out on monday the second of the month if the weather is good and Shall see you in the course of the week, in the mean time I wish you every happiness, and am your friend —

Pren

P.S. I hope M<sup>r</sup> Casdebel, whom I have not heard from since my absence, will have the returns ready by my arrival
P

Joseph Howell Esq<sup>t</sup>

Dear Sir

Your favor of the 30<sup>th</sup> of November last only reached me this day — enclos'd are two letters one from M<sup>r</sup> White, the other from some friend of yours from the Southward — this was shar'd on me, but on looking at the name Conceive it was of a private nature, and therefore immediately deliver'd — M<sup>r</sup> White has sent by this post 1300 [undecipherable] Number of his register — the printer still goes on rapidly by M<sup>r</sup> [undecipherable] informs me that his business will be compleated in the time contracted for — at his request and the report of M<sup>r</sup> William Francis to the board of Treasury that M<sup>r</sup> Follet has accomplished one fourth part of his Contract for which his pay going to day him — think you would adopt some mode to have the registers of the Virginia Line launch, for the press will be delayed —

I had wrote this for, when M<sup>r</sup> Anderset presented me with the letter noticed, marks this + by the hand writing Supposed it to be your M<sup>r</sup> Parkins consequently a private one —

I have received letters from Brown & Markland, and from the printer at Richmond, acknowledging me the receipt of yours with the receipt of my part —

I am happy to inform you that the business of the Office does not require your attendance — The Compliments of the Gentlemen of the Office wait on you, and they if you would accept of the best wishes of one who has the happiness to subscribe himself

Jos Howell

John Pierce Esq<sup>r</sup>

William [undecipherable] — these Gentlemen were of the Quota of your State, and the Certificates obtained agme from Major [undecipherable] in consequence of some charges the State of Maryland had against them those charges. Maryland credits these Gentlemen and their Certificates in [undecipherable] things as you will observe by the enclosed Account, which I thought proper to transmit for the information of the [undecipherable] concerned — You will please to transmit me a receipt for the Certificates — I thank you for your attention in forwarding my request, and by a continuance and [undecipherable] of your favors —

M<sup>r</sup>. [undecipherable] arrived on [undecipherable] day last and [undecipherable] to [undecipherable] your friends around you, and believe me to be with the utmost sincerity your friend
Jos. Howell

[John White]
37627:
West Point 23 Decemr 1785                                19

S<sup>ir</sup>

I notice you on y<sup>e</sup> 9<sup>th</sup> Instant that I had put by M<sup>r</sup> Isaac Garrison one Hundred & fifteen Maggan Covers, but he denied me about the Iepsle, and this is the first Opportunity that has offered Since they being for Batley that Could not sent them. Be. I send my Papel notify the Same to Christine — they will now be delivered by William Curtis, for which the freight is paid —

Since I have had Charge of the Men at this Place, three have Deserted, five from Connecticut and two as Guard at Constitution Island, and five from the Gaberkes, I have Sent Parties out after them —

I am S<sup>r</sup>
William Rice
[The Honourable                                             William Williams
Major Gen<sup>l</sup> Knox Sec<sup>y</sup> at War —]

---

West Point 6<sup>th</sup> Jany 1786 —

S<sup>ir</sup>

I now forward the returns for the month of Decemr 1785.
I wrote you on y<sup>e</sup> 9<sup>th</sup> [strikethrough: of] of Decemr [18?] the Propriety of a Physician to come and attend the Sick at this place — there is some Sick here at present but I have not Called when any One to attend them at York —

One of the recruiting Serjeants in Connecticut has but one more man & the is unfit for a Soldier by reason of a Continual running from whence Present So that he Cannot wear his Accoutrements, he was Despit say by Baron Stiben upon the Same Account for four years — I Shall not give him any Clothing till you Indicate — he is very inclined to be Discharged and his excuse nothing from the Public —

I am S<sup>r</sup> William Rice

[The Honourable Major Gen Knox
Secretary at War —]
