38004:
Port Pit. July 3. 1786.

Sir, One John Bull (called by the Indian Sheke) informs me that he left detroit on the 29<sup>th</sup> of April, in company with One hundred [undecipherable] Men, women & children, and are at present about forty miles from Cuyahoga. that they have formed the design and intention to settle near their old towns on the Muskingum, who if part of the Six Nations, were ready to assemble but as they are too late in the Season for planting, intend building and making their huts at Cuyahoga until fall.

He further informs me, that on the 7<sup>th</sup> Ultimo Company of Indians viz Captains M<sup>r</sup> Simon Girty and Mathew Elliott with forty Indians, among whom were the half King, Baubee, Moon, and Bald Egant, a clan of the Ottaway Nations, who lately joined the Mouth of the Sandusky River for Canada. They announced that they were going as far as Quebec and to be supplied with lands on the North side of the lake from the Indians but it was generally supposed they were going to a new home Government would give them. Sir John Johnson (who was then) had invited them and the Six Nations to.

These Indian Nations (among whom Bull resides) I am told I can trust as far as ever being friendly to the United States.

I am, your most Obedient Servant
Jos. Harmar

[Samuel Knox, Secretary at War.]

38014:
Your Commissioners on a Consideration of the Contradictory evidence given by the same persons, is of Opinion that the Arrangement on which states his resignation to be on the first of January 1781 ought to be Considered as binding because the Certificate of Col<sup>o</sup> Elbert ascertains only that he did not resign in 1778 but says nothing as to his Resignation in 1781 and the other Evidences go only to a belief of his Continuance in Service.

Your Commissioners is further of Opinion that the proof on which this resignation was grounded which must have been Affirmative ought to appear, that a proper Judgement may be formed of its Validity before the same can be set a side.

38055:
Boston July 25. 1786

Gentlemen

I arrived here this Morning before Jean having left New York on Saturday afternoon. The Board may remember that the Man who is now in Goal has said that a Major Morris who Acted as Agent to N Hampshire line could Clear up his Character and he expected him soon to do it. Inclosed is the Copy of a deposition of Judge Sullivan, who suspects the Major as being the Person who Counterfeited the Certificates. I am therefore induced to Persue my Journey to Morrow Morning on to N Hampshire instead of returning to N York which Imeditated to do it by setting out in to Morrow stage. The Varrous depositions taken here amounts to but little more than to prove that Barnes is a Villain and ought to be taken up. M<sup>r</sup> Lovitt in Goal can inform where is it to be found. I shall obtain a direct deposition that Barnes sold these Certificates as good and send it on. If possible by this post I have taken up the Man who is to give it and he never on tryal. Phelon has been gone some where fourteen days his Landlady where he lodged which was 8 Miles from this Supposes that he is gone to Providence or New York and from thence to N York. I have sent after him with proper directions to send him on, and now inclose the depositions on which the first Justice's warrant is founded, that in Case he should get to N York before the Warrant he may be Apprehended in the mean time. I shall on to Paternosk, I have taken the Liberty to write to the Board for the reason that I conclude Judge Morris is from town on the Circuit — I conclude Judge Morris is from

N.B. The Board will excuse the haste of the Letter as I am in the hurry of Coach & things

Honorable Board of Treasury        J B

38059:
New York July 25<sup>th</sup> 1786.

Sir,

Your favor of the 19th instant was received Yesterday, and agreeably to your request have made inquiry of the Commissr for the M<sup>r</sup> Cantd Commissaries department (M<sup>r</sup> Brenall) what was necessary to be done — he informs me that your accounts come properly under his notice; that he has the charges of Carpenter Wharton against his deputies, and expects those against you are with him — the time limited for the accounts does not in the least apply to M<sup>r</sup> Brenalls department. You can therefore settle your accounts when your Business will most conveniently admit; tho it will be necessary that you attend here for the purpose; and my advice is if the object is worthy your attention that you will do it as soon as may be —

Yours

J. Howell

M<sup>r</sup> Sam<sup>l</sup> Dilworth

38064:
[88]

Gentlemen                    Boston July 26<sup>th</sup> 1786

I did myself the honor to write very hastily to the board yesterday and inform them that I proposed to go to Portſmouth to day, but having taken up Simeon Turner for Paſſing the Certificates he bought of Barnes Knowing them to be Counterfeit and a Court being held upon him I was not ready soon enough for the Stage and am therefore Obliged to wait untill Friday. I hope this will have no ill Effect as I have Carefully concealed that I have Suſpicions of any Persons not in Town & my exertions to get here has thrown me into a Slight Fever, which made it necessary to indulge a day for repose, Turner being Acquitted I have taken his depoſition which I enclose together with that of Tudor (with the Note,) with whom it Stopped from every information I am induced strongly to believe that these counterfeits came from the way of New Hampshire.

July 27<sup>th</sup> The first day I did not appear in Town. I sent after Phelon immediately. his Land lady said that he was gone to Providence in Rhode Island and Perhaps might go to New York. I then sent and wrote to Rhode Island who has returned without any intelligence of him, as he may have gone to N York I sent the

38083:
New York 1 August 1786

Sir,

On examination of the rolls of the late 3<sup>rd</sup> Penn<sup>a</sup> I find Francis Farren is included for his pay to the 1 October 1778 — from which time he is left out, and no reason assigned, the musters of that Regiment for September being with you they may present you with the inform- ation necessary — The rolls of the late 12 Reg. from time of Enlistment are not at present in my possession therefore cannot inform you the time of Farren's entering the service — he is on the pay rolls for May 1777.

I wish your attention to mine of the 10 July, relative to the musters of the 8<sup>th</sup> Reg. — being much in want of them —

I am &

J. Howell

J<sup>no</sup> Nicholson
38084:
New York August 4<sup>th</sup> 1786.

Sir,

In answer to yours of the 20 July, I have to inform you that M<sup>r</sup> Joseph Cox of the Late 6<sup>th</sup> regiment, has been settled with as a supernumerary Officer in consequence of his being arranged in 1778, there were no Officers in his situation allowed Commutation, excepting a few which the State adopted, and were recommended by Gen<sup>l</sup> M<sup>c</sup>Clalan, this was not done in the case of M<sup>r</sup> Cox consequently his Claim for Commutation is inadmissable.

I return you the order of John Smith, as the pay in Pension, claimed by him as not drawn could not have been charged him by the State in account of depreciation, consequently he has received the value of it.

M<sup>r</sup> Binn is absent from this Office, and not expected for some time; on his return I will present your letter with Manning's discharge, and if any thing can be done for him I will inform you —

Yours &<sup>c</sup>

J. Howell Jr.

[Gen<sup>l</sup> R. Thompson.]

N York Aug. 7<sup>th</sup> 1786.

Sir,

Not knowing until yesterday where to direct to you has prevented me from answering your several letters sooner — I have now enclosed you an account of the monies which are charged you in the books of this Office for which it will be necessary to produce sufficient vouchers for the expenditure before you can be exonerated —

The monies you have received from Major Einnion Williams appears to me to have been for recruiting your Company in the State Regiments — If this should be the case I am of opinion the accounts should be settled by M<sup>r</sup> Nicholson, who I suppose has the returns of that regiment previous to its being incorporated with the line — You had therefore best inform yourself of M<sup>r</sup> Nicholson previous to a settlement at this Office — I wish you to bring every paper that will give information to form a settlement.

Yours &

Joseph Howell jr

Boston August 7 1786

Cap<sup>t</sup> John Phelen who has been employed by me as a Confidential Clerk, and who is strongly suspected has fraudulently taken a quantity of the Jences Settlements Certificate Papers on which the debt of the United States is Certifyed, which will be of infinite disservice to the United States, as well as an injury to my Character.

I have no doubt but that you will give every assistance in your power in apprehending the said Phelon and transmitting him to the Authority of the State of New York for his Tryal. The Bearer

will have the honor to deliver this Letter —

M<sup>r</sup> Chittenden
of Vermont

Sir          New York, Office of Army Acc<sup>ts</sup> Aug 28. 1786

Major Whitcomb who Commanded an In-dependant Corps of Rangers, having made an Application to me for the Pay & depreciation due to Sundry Soldiers who Served in his Corps and who belonging to that district of Country Called, the New Hampshire Grants or Vermont, have not been Considered as of the quota either of New York or N. Hampshire — as the depreciation of the Army by the Resolutions of Congress is to be made up by the Respective States to which the Soldiers belong, I do not find myself Authorized to Contradict that System, and therefore have done myself the Honor to request, if it is proper an Exception should take place in this Instance that I may be directed Accordingly.

I am —
President of Congress —               J.P.

38126:
as I had no Evidence against Gray except a Similarity of hand Writing and his Connexion with Morrell he was of course Acquitted.

Had I not happily proceeded according to the advice and Opinion of the President of the State and the principal Characters in it, I should undoubtedly been Arrested for false imprisonment of Gray or been otherwise personally insulted, and it was with some difficulty that their influence prevailed to prevent.

I shall stay here until Monday, my reasons for which is that I shall most probably collect information which may be of Service, which I have not been able to do so fully as I kept myself unknown as much as possible when in Town before.

I hope Norris may be kept from receiving directions from Morrell, who undoubtedly will Attempt to prevent his deposition. for he appeared on his examination more Solicitous about this than other Evidence which might be produced against him,     I have the Honor to be

Dear Sir          New York August 25, 1786

Your Letter of the 17<sup>th</sup> came to hand Yesterday. your hint respect to Monies omitted to be stopped from and over drawn by the Officers I am Oblig<sup>d</sup> to you for and will thank you to make out a list of Such sums as come to Your Knowledge and transmit the Same to me. we are taking Measures to institute Actions against such persons as must refund Money. but untill that is done am not able to give you or any person instructions on the Subject.

Since my report to the Secretary at War ascertaining the Ballance due to the Officers in Specie on Acct of their Pay I have not had any agency in that buisness when the Money is furnished I shall take the proper Steps to get it into their hands. but from the present Situation of our finances I have but little hopes of that being done

Andrew Dunscomb Es<sup>q</sup>          Sam<sup>l</sup> dir [PP]
Richmond

Philadelphia August 25. 1785.

My dear General.

The Gentleman who honors this letter with his care is M<sup>r</sup> Tharp of Jamaica — he is on a visit to the Continent for the recovery of his health — and to promote that valuable object he purposes a tour through the eastern States with a view to render his residence in Boston as agreable as possible, and in order to produce the fairest report of our country, I beg leave to introduce him to the honor of your acquaintance and to request for him your best civilities while in Boston — he has been sometime a fellow-lodger with me — and I am authorised, as well from my own observation, as the report of his respectable friends, to assure you of meeting in M<sup>r</sup> Tharp the polite Gentleman and agreable acquaintance.

The packet with which M<sup>r</sup> Tharp is so good as to charge himself is forwarded at the request of our State Society — and would have been sent sooner had any opportunity so good as the present conveyance offered.

You will oblige me my dear General, by making M<sup>r</sup> Tharp acquainted with your friends, to [underline: all of whom] I offer my most respectful remembrance. — Be pleased to present my affectionate respects to M<sup>rs</sup> Lincoln and the family — and be persuaded of the truth with which I am ever yours

W Jackson

[Nov<sup>r</sup> Elackfon
Aug<sup>t</sup> 25
1785]

The Board of Treasury to whom was referred the Petition of James Burnside, Doyle Swney and Robert Underwood, late Clerks to the Commissioners for Settling accounts of the Hospital and Clothing Departments —

Beg leave to Report.

That the Resolves of Congress of March 9<sup>th</sup> 1786. Establishing a new System for Settling the Accounts of the five great Departments, having fixed a period of three Weeks for the termination of the Business under the former Commissioners; the principal part of the Books & Papers belonging to the respective Offices might, in the opinion of the Board, have been got ready for delivery, previous to the 15 day of April last; and that at all events, the furnishing more than one [undecipherable] warrant necessary to complete this object after that period.

That on this account the Board conceived themselves authorized to make an extra Allowance to the Principals of the Department of a Months Salary, presuming that from this allowance compensation would be made by the Commissioners to their respective Clerks, who it is to be supposed were principally employed in the Business of preparing, delivering the Books and Papers of their several Office —

That no such compensation has not been made by the Several Commissioners, and as the principal Business for which it is claimed, has been done by their Clerks (whose Salaries are not more than is necessary for their actual Subsistence) The Board Submit to the Consideration of Congress, the following Resolve —

That there be allowed to each of the Commissioners for Settling the Accounts of the five great Departments, the Salary of one Clerk from the 15 April last, to the time when the Books and Papers of the respective Departments were delivered over to the present Commissioners —

All which is humbly Submitted
Sign<sup>d</sup>    Samuel Osgood

On my return to Boston from New Hampshire I was informed that John Phelen the Confidential Clerk in my Office who is suspected to have stolen some of my Certificate Papers &to have Countersigled thereon the finances of the United States, was seen on the road which leads thro the New Hampshire grants and that he had intimated to some of his friends that he was going to Canada — which
38158:
New York August 30. 1796

on the 4<sup>th</sup> instant I persued Captain John Pleles agaist your Instructions — I took the rout to Crown Point by the way of N U — at Lenned in Massachusetts I was informed by a Captain Bliss, who was perfectly acquainted with Pleles that he passed thro' town on Sunday the 4<sup>th</sup> of July which was the evening he absconded — at Littleton a town about twelves miles from Lenned he had stoped the play to night, but also owned the greatest anxiety to get forward pleading for new cases that he had a brother sick at Belfast, which required his immediate presence — This information I had from one Widow in Farmers Harger where he lodged & who appeared to me to be an intelligent man.

I received no information that I could depend on after this untill I arrivied at Lake Champlain which was not untill the 20<sup>th</sup> my journey being extremely impeded by bad roads & the circuitous route I was obliged to take to gain intelligence. after my being at Lake Champlain five days, I was informed not at Colchester Point a surer order over from there to Canada (with a horse) fully convincing the discription of Capt Pleles however there are seven leavens for I am fully of opinion that he has gone to Quebec or to some other place in Canada —
I am Sir with the greatest regret

38195:
[Report]                    Office of Army Accounts
                                        New York Septem 19. 1786

The Commissioner of Army Accounts to whom was referred the Petition of Daniel Pendleton late a Captain of Artificieres begs leave to report

That the Objects of the Petition are;

1<sup>st</sup> an allowance as Paymaster & Clothier to his Company,

2<sup>d</sup> an allowance as Agent on the final Settlement of the Accounts of his Company, and

3<sup>d</sup> Half pay for life or Commutation in Lieu of it.

On the first Article your Commissioner has to Observe the duties of Paymaster & Clothier were executed by the Petitioner for his Company which in Equity may deserve some Compensation, but as there is no precedent of any such allowance & as there have been Several Captains in the Army Commanding Separate or detached Corps who have executed these duties as there is no existing resolve of Congress that would Counton an award a recompence the Commissioner therefore Supposes that an allowance to M<sup>r</sup> Pendleton would be improper unless Congress think it Adviseable to Establish a general rule for all Cases of this Nature.

In respect to the Petitioners Second request, which is an Allowance to him as Agent to his Corps. Your Commissioner is of Opinion that he is intitled to the Same, and that the resolve of the 6 Instant has provided for his Case.

Your Commissioner further begs Leave to
38200:

New York September 14 1786

Sir

As to the particular Stating of your Brother Acco<sup>t</sup> is either Mislaid or with M<sup>r</sup> Wirant in Georgia, I am unable to give him that Satisfaction which I otherwise could and which my wishes lead me to. I shall however take up the principles of it & State them as accurately as possible: in general I can say that his Account was reduced to the same rule of allowances with the others of the line and the Charges in it admitted upon the Same principles, his Charge of pay for himself as Surgeon to a regiment was wholly admitted. his Charge of rations was also admitted. from which was deducted one p<sup>r</sup>day as received for a reason that a resolution of Congress has directed, that if this ration was not drawn it should not be Charged, and that as there was no check to the Accounts of the line, I conceived I should not be Justified in passing the whole rations without such a deduction. the officers in general must have received a ration at least while in Camp. If when out of Camp or furlough, it is doubted rather that allowance was intended to be made them. The State allowed three rations at a quarter of a dollar, but I only allowed for a part of the 9<sup>o</sup> a Sp<sup>d</sup> at 9<sup>2</sup> and a part a P<sup>d</sup> go agreeable to the resolves of Congress which makes a very Considerable alteration in your Account; the pay & Rations of the Servants of the Officers I only admitted from the time Congress auth<sup>or</sup>ized
38203:
Dear Sir

New York September 14. 1786.

I hope you will consider as a sufficient excuse that I have delayed answering your favor of the 2 instant, in order that I may give you a better Opinion on the subject of it.

I am of Opinion that the charges for bounty Authenticated in the Manner of the inclosed Papers, is in sufficient that you and the other State Commissioners must be either furnished with the Muster and an Account of the bounty paid for by the United States from my Office, or the Acco<sup>t</sup> must be sent to me and Checked. in this Opinion are the People in Office, with the most of whom I have consulted.

The Resolves of Congress of April 10. 1780 and May 15. 1783. with several Others sufficiently shew the uniform sense of Congress respecting the Principles of the depreciation both in respect to the Continental Army & the Militia. I suppose that the States were Authorized during the times of the Old Money times to pay in that currency only and that nothing but Particular Resolves, can warrant the making up a depreciation on this money in any instance whatever. the State therefore must produce this Authority and where they cannot shew it, their Accounts cannot be allowed. of this Opinion are the Commissioners of the Treasury who have Accordingly written to M<sup>r</sup> Gorham signifying their disapprobation of his Conduct.

I suppose that the general rule that

38212:
the Officers and Men Voluntarily gave Credit for the Same on their Settlements. M<sup>r</sup> Hockley having this day also Exhibited his Account of these advances to me in Order to be Obtain a discharge for the Same, and it appearing to me improper that the United States should bear any loss in Consequence of the failure of the State in making the Stoppages or that they should be Chargeable with this Money at a greater Value than the State has deducted the Same from the Line, I have done myself the honor to address the Board, and to request that if it is proper the State may be either called upon to Exhibite the Acct of Stoppages made for this money or that the whole Sum Should be charged at once to the State after deducting sums as have been accounted for or now on the Settlements of the Line. I am induced to propose this the more readily because this is one of the States that has taken on it self an unauthorized to settle with its Line for the Year 1781, and which has neglected to Call on the Officers of the United States for any advances —

Honorable
The Board of Treasury

Sam J

I am informed that John Phelon, the Person I was in pursuit of has returned and taken up in Boston as by his return may he Concerted measures to brave any [undecipherable: Enquiry or] trial which may be made respecting him, I have taken [strikethrough: therfore] the Liberty to Inclose you the depositions tho the Suspicions of him arose, and they do not amount to such Evidence as will Convict him, yet I am of Opinion that his Knowledge of the Checks of the Office and his Capacity to do Mischief is such as will induce your Government to take Exceeding good Care of him to prevent the ill Consequences in which he is guilty — and his hiding the papers or his matters evidences the fear of a Search. Major Story: Report:      Office of Army Accounts Sept 23. 1786       The Commissioner for Settling the Accounts of the Late Army, to whom was referred the Petition of George Thompson, Executor to the Estate of the Late General William Thompson, begs Leave to report that the Petitioners demand for a return of the General from the 3d day of June 1776 the time of his being made a prisoner to the 3d day of September 1781 when he died is attended with some difficulties, the principal of which is that the General Officers of the Army, not being Confined to one ration daily as was the Case with the Regimental Officers, have drawn from the different Commissaries such quantity of provision on Account as they found necessary for the Supply of their respective Tables, that but few if any of the these Gentlemen ever kept any regular Accounts of their drafts upon the Commissaries and that the Accounts of that department as will appear by Mr Dennalls Letter to your Commissioner accompanying this report are in such

38251: the Public advanced to him but above the probable recruting expences of the Regiment and therefore unless the same can be recovered must be a loss either to him or the United States in support to his claim for this balance he urges that he intrusted this money as was the duty of the Commanding Officer of a Regiment, to his Officers for the Public Service and for whose conduct he cannot be answerable as they were not of his appointment: but let the full force of this reasoning be granted and that he was perfectly Justifiable in making these advances in the first instance, was it not his duty to give some of the public Officers a knowledge of these advances to enable them to make the [undecipherable] & was it not his duty to pay such attention to the expenditures for the recruting service as to prevent so considerable a loss on the recruting Account of his Regiment? the Officers to whom he paid this money may be considered as under several views. such as died in the service such as resigned and such as had depreciation due to them or continued to the end of the War he may say in respect to the first that he had no opportu[nity] to call them to account which reasoning undoubtedly ought to have weight— but still as he knew the sum they had received and the Number of Men inlisted by them it appears no more than reasonable that he should [have]

38256:
New York October 7, 1786

Your Letter of the 26<sup>th</sup> ult<sup>o</sup> has been received. I am of Opinion that your reasoning is founded in the principles of Justice, but find that the allowances you propose to make are not Conformable to the resolve of Congress which the Board of Treasury are of Opinion you are only guided by, from which no Commissioners can depart. it appears to be the proper to the Board that the States Accounts should be reduced to this standard on the first instance, &the variations from is admitted when in Congress, a by some Appointments for that Purpose, when all those Cases of Equity are finally brought together, when we examine the resolutions &respecting allowances to accounts which join the Army. I find them the same as the Militia. it may not be reasonable to allow the Latter a Compensation in expenses farther than has been always paid to the former. &more especially as the expence in travelling could not be greater.

Royall Flint Esquire.
Sam<sup>tn</sup> H

38265:
New York October 7, 1786

Your Letter of the [undecipherable] has been received. I am of Opinion that your reasoning is founded on the Principles of Justice, but I find that the allowances you propose to make are not Conformable to the resolves of Congress, which the Board of Treasury are of Opinion are your only guides, & from which no Commissioner can depart. It appears to be the design to the Board that the States Accounts shall be reduced to this standard on the final settlement. The variation from is admitted when in Congress, & by some Appointments for that Purpose, when all those Cases of Equity are finally brought together, when we examine the resolutions respecting allowances to accounts which join the Army. I find them the same as the Militia, it may not be reasonable to allow the latter a Compensation, an expence farther than has been always paid to the former. I most especially as the expence in travelling could not be greater.

Royall Flint Esquire.
I am Sir &<sup>c</sup>

[168] Report       Office of Army Accounts
March 10. 1786.

The Commissioner for Settling the Accounts of the late Army to whom was referred a Letter from Major Gen<sup>l</sup> S. Llair, begs Leave to report —

That the General Officers of the Army were not confined in their drafts to any particular quantity of Rations or Commissary, and therefore your Commissioners conceive it to be improper to settle with them without a Collection of their Accounts from the Commissaries departments, or that the quantities they have received should be ascertained in some other Manner.

The Commissioner does himself the honor to Enclose and Extract of a Letter from M<sup>r</sup> Burral shewing the difficulty of Checking those Accounts in any of the Public Offices. He therefore begs Leave to Submit, the propriety of the General Officers rendering and Accurate Accounts of what quantities & Species of the Articles they have received from the Public from time to time in the Supplies of their respective tables and Attesting to the same prior to the Admisfion of their demands for Arrears of that Nature.

Charles Thomson S<sup>y</sup>. S<sup>y</sup> Congress.

38290:
[War Office. respecting Officers
pay Subsistence & forage
Re
21 Oct[undecipherable] 179[undecipherable]]

War Office, October 21, 1796

Sir

In obedience to the act of Congress of the 26<sup>th</sup> Instant, which you will receive from the Secretary I have the honor to inform your Excellency, that the proportion of commissioned officers for the two hundred and sixty men to be raised in New Hampshire will be as follows

One Major
Four Captains
Two Lieutenants
Four Ensigns
One Surgeon
One Surgeons Mate

I enclose the establishment of hay subsistence and forage and the existing articles of War to your Excellency, in order for your information and the information of the officers who may be appointed.

Arrangements will be taken to furnish complete uniform clothing arms and accoutrements

and one months pay to the officers and men previous to their marching and also for the supply of rations.

It has been usual in the recruiting service as an inducement for men to enlist, to advance at the time of being sworn, two dollars or half a months pay. If your Excellency will please to make an arrangement for the instant supply of this money, and some small advances for fuel, straw and barrack utensils, it shall be replaced to you in a short period.

I have enclosed recruiting instructions for the senior officer, whose name you will have the goodness to insert.

Oeconomy will dictate that the rendezvous of the troops should be as few as possible, at one, or at most two places.

As soon as your Excellency shall have made the appointments I request that you would transmit them to this office, and their Commissions shall be

forwarded as soon as possible.

I have the honor to be
With the highest respect,
Your Excellencys
Most obedient
Humble Servant
Knox

His Excellency
The President
of New Hampshire

New York 22 October 1786

My dear Wadsworth

You will see by the acts of Congres of the 20<sup>th</sup> instant, which are transmitted to Governor Huntington by this Post, the high instability of an Indian War.

As a Lieutenant Colonel Comm[and] is apportioned on Connecticut. Propose to your friends if it can be done without injuring any other officer of superior rank, who may be desirous of entering the Service, and endeavor to have poor Wylles apprised of it.

I know not how this might operate in case of further additions to the troops now ordered to be raised, and therefore would not urge the measure to the injury of the Service. — as to Major Wyllerhaps, in my opinion much rest on ambition life, and as he is an excellent officer I wish that he may be promoted whenever it can be done with propreity — I have not forgotten the Philantail [undecipherable] encouragement. I have expected to be there

here with comps of this week, and if the goods you wished from thence are of the proper kind, they shall be sent to you at Hartford

with perfect affection
and esteem

and humble Servant

38304:
A Talk deliverd by the Kings, Head men and Warriors of the Creek Nation, to the Commissioners for holding a Conference and Treaty with this Indians, by the General Assembly of the State of Georgia near the mouth of Shoulderbone on the Oconee River Octob<sup>r</sup> 22<sup>d</sup> 1786 —

Fathers, Friends, & Brothers —

We the head men have come to answer your Talk which we heard yesterday, and to tell you that we are willing to give you Satisfaction for the Injuries our people have done to the State of Georgia — There are white people in the nation who have been the Occasion of those murders, they must and will be killd when we return to the Nation and the Indians who were Concerned must also be killd — two Indians were killd when the mischief was done. The Chief person who has been acquainted with you is Alexander McGilwry he is of a large family, therefore we will give him an opportunity of going away quietly, but we must remove him if he Chooses to live with the Spaniards he is obliged to them, or else he must also be killd. We do not mean to deceive you —

Samuel Barrett Esquire

Philadelphia November 1796

Dear Sir.

I hope in another Post or two at farthest to transmit you what you have requested, and what I should have done long ago — my acquire, thang of business must be my excuse — There is a Certificate for 1500 dollars N<sup>o</sup> 89362 in the Name of James Armstrong applied to be paid into the Land Office the party claims it as being the Original property of James Armstrong of Lee's Legion his being settled in Carolina makes me scrupulous and yet the circumstances make it probable to him could you by next post drop me a line who this James Armstrong for whom the above Certificate was made out is

I am &c —

Joseph Howell J<sup>r</sup> Esq<sup>r</sup>.          J<sup>no</sup>. Nicholson

New York November 11<sup>th</sup> 1786

Dear Sir,

Your letter of the 4<sup>th</sup> instant to M<sup>r</sup> Pierce and the one of the 5<sup>th</sup> to me was duly received, apprehending you have not received my letter of the 18<sup>th</sup> Ultimo I take the liberty to inclose a duplicate as also an Extract of my letter of the same date to M<sup>r</sup> Nicholson on the subject of your Claim— You will please to wait on M<sup>r</sup> Nicholson who will shew you the pay Roll alluded to in my letters which I presume will fully satisfy you that Colonel Haas was accountable to his Officers for the balance of pay & due them and their Men and that the United States are entirely exonerated from any and all demands which the Regiment under his command might have had against them— I am &c.

Joseph Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>

Col<sup>l</sup> Will<sup>m</sup> Williams

I am therefore of Opinion that it was expected that the Militia Privates should carry their own Packs of Clothes as well as the Continental Soldiers and that the only allowance which can be made them while Marching and before they drawn Provisions is one dollar & one third Provets under the Resolve of Congress of October 12 1775 which I find the Pay Officers of Congress have allowed the Militia by the term of billeting money.

My dear Sir I write you by Capt [undecipherable] — [undecipherable] late I am uncertain what measures the board of treasury will finally adopt. They have advertised in this days paper for a contract for provisions. Something must soon be determined on. All I hope is that such measures may be adopted as will most effectually promote the public Service

In addition to the terms proposed when you were here, it seems to be the idea of some gentlemen of [undecipherable] that [undecipherable] the contracts for the troops, a further sum of 100,000 dollars should be—
[undecipherable]

paid by the contractors in quarterly instalments, to the Treasurer of the United States for the civil list— The obligation or orders on the receivers would then have on unlimited operation throughout all the States— You may remember that when you were here it was proposed that some States should be reserved for this purpose— Mr Morris was fully of opinion that it was preferable to pay the money for the civil list, instead of assessing any of the States

It would be unnecessary to enlarge on these points, as Mr Knox will soon be at Hartford from whom you will have the fullest information

I am my dear Sir
Your very affectionate
Knox

— Col° Wadsworth

New York 29. November 1786

My dear Wadsworth — Mr King will have explained every thing, and I hope to your Satisfaction.

You will receive by this post a letter from the board of treasury, and the terms of the proposed contracts, which I have sent to Mr Morris. A similar letter will be written to him tomorrow. On the receipt of his answer in the affirmative it will be absolutely necessary for you to repair to Philadelphia in order to make your private arrangements with each other. I sincerely hope that you will not find any insurmountable difficulty in making this journey without which the affair cannot be completed.

I pray you to present my compliments to Mrs Wadsworth & the young ladies, and also to Mrs & Mr King.

I am my dear
Your affectionate
Humble Servant

38390:
Fort Harmar. December 6. 1786.

Brothers:

The honorable Congress have been pleased to pass the enclosed resolve in your favor. I <sup>have</sup> [strikethrough: am] directed that the Corn and other articles shall be sent down to this post, where they will be ready to be delivered to you. In obedience to the Orders of Congress, I have to inform you that that honorable body are well pleased to hear of your arrival, and have granted you permission to return to your former settlements on the Muskingum, where you may be assured of the friendship and protection of the United States.

I should wish to know the names of the principal men who have the direction of your affairs, and shall be happy in rendering you every assistance in my power.

I am, Brothers, your friend.

Jos. Harmar.
Lt. Col. Com'd &c

[To the Moravian Indians at or near Cuyahoga. —]

Sir

The Assembly of this State have directed the Regimental Accounts to acc<sup>t</sup> with the Com<sup>tee</sup> of Pay Table for the final settlement Certificates which they rec<sup>d</sup> from you. I find that I shall want Copies of their Rec<sup>ts</sup> & possibly a Copy of your Register so far as respects the Certificates issued to the Conn<sup>t</sup> Line, I must therefore request that you would transmit them as soon as convenient —

Oliver Wolcott Esq<sup>r</sup> N York Dec<sup>r</sup> 15. 1786

Dear sir

I think at present that you had best send to me all the Vouchers and receipts received by you from M<sup>r</sup> Thomas, reserving only in your hands the final settlement Certificates you received from him, of which Certificates I wish you to send me a list and a receipt. I am your Office to Account for the same to the United States either by a delivering to the Persons to whom they belong or to be deposited in the same Manner as the other Certificates which shall be delivered in to you by the Agents of the Line. M<sup>r</sup> King will want the Vouchers for his Settlements, and the Certificates ought to remain in the State ready for the Claimants.

In the Sept last I inclosed to the Governor of your State, Copies of the receipts of the agents of your Line which I requested he would deliver over to the Officer appointed to settle their Accounts. I informed him also that an Acct was permitted to the Register of my Certificates now in the hands of the Loan Officer of your State, for the Purpose of Checking the Agents Accounts. I must therefore request that you will be pleased to Call on those Gentlemen for those receipts and Registers, and to inform me, if you find you fail in obtaining them or should they not have been
38403:
New York December 13<sup>th</sup> 1780

Dear Sir

Inclosed are the vouchers you transmitted to me relative to the charges in Col<sup>o</sup> White's account. They are returned because his accounts are to be acted on by him with the State. The letter to him I will thank you to deliver on his arrival at Richmond. It contains his accounts and vouchers — Cap<sup>t</sup> Frances Mentie having settled his account of old monies with you and not accounted for the whole he received extra a considerable sum I have looked and sent his account as it stands in my Books and also in your settlement — I observe also that he charges his extra pay as Aid de Camp while prisoner. you know that the pay of an Officer as Commission never ceases until he is brought to a legal discharge, but the extra pay of a Staff appointment is only given for certain temporary services performed which ceases with the services and an Officer as soon as made a Prisoner being incapacitated from services cannot therefore be intitled to an extra pay. his place was therefore immediately filled by some other Officer where the service was still necessary where not the Office and its appendages consequently cease. I wish you therefore to inform me of the reasons of this charge and the propriety of its admittance. I am &<sup>c</sup>

Andrew Dunscomb Esq<sup>r</sup>                    J. Pierce
38404:
New york Novem<sup>r</sup> 17 1796

Sir,

Your Letter of the 17 July last covering an Account for expences on immanuel &c was duly received—

The money you advanced for Inlisting the Soldiers when on their march to join the Regiment, I am willing to allow — provided you making it appear that no part of those Sums were received from the public on account of Subsistance & that no rations were drawn for them during the time.

The expences amounting to £26. 10 Inlisting between this & the 77<sup>d</sup>. & Col. W<sup>s</sup>. [undecipherable] being as I conceive for recruiting and collecting clothing appears to be improper as Col Sherburne drew the Subsistance of the Officers while on this Recruiting Service and on settlement of his Account has produced your Receipt for the Sum of £20. 14 — being so much due you for while on the Recruiting Service — the latter of your Services appear to have been performed at the same time, therefore payment cannot be granted but for on Duty—

I am y<sup>r</sup>

[undecipherable] [undecipherable]

[S. H. Kasin]
38405:
New York, Dcember 15, 1786

Sir

Your letter of the 4<sup>th</sup> July last covering an Account for expences on command &c was duly received —

The money you advanced for subsisting the soldiers when on their march to join the Regiment I am willing to allow provided you make it appear that no part of those sums were received for the Public on account of subsistance and that no rations were drawn for them during the time — The expences amounting to $18.10 Ninings between the 1 October 77 and December 18 following being as is expressed for recruiting and assisting coastings appear to be improper as Col: Sherburne drew the Subsistance of the Officers while on the Recruiting Service and on settlement of his Accounts has produced your receipt for the sum of $18.14 being so much due you while on the Recruiting service — The latter and former services appear to have been performed at the same time therefore payment cannot be granted twice for one Duty —

I am &c

Lieut Seth Chapin
J Pierce

38410:
Office of Army Accounts

New York Dec 10<sup>th</sup> 1786

Gentlemen

When I advancd an estimate of the Specie
have due to the Virginia Officers I was not satisfied of the actual
settlements made with that line since which they have been
received and entered in my Books, that return therefore was made
out on the Supposition that the Specie pay of 1782 & 1783 was
charged to the Officers whether received or not, and that all advances
were deducted from his accounts but I find on a careful
revision of those accounts that there are several instances of
departure from those principles and therefore it became
necessary to revise my return on the usual closing of the same
to the sums as they stand in the one now enclosed —
I do myself the Honor also to enclose a return of the names
of such Officers as have drawn their Pay without accounting
for monies or else which they had received &consequently who
have a balance against them to be refunded to the
public unless indeed they should have demands for means
of subsistence which arrears I believe cannot be ascertained
with any Official certainty —

I am &c
[signature: Pierce]

Honorable
Board of Treasury

38412:
[203]

[bag]        Office of Army Accounts

New York December 10<sup>th</sup> 1785

Sir

On the 10<sup>th</sup> day of January last I had the Honor to lay before you as compleat a Return of the Specie due to the Officers of the Virginia Line as the evidence then in my Possession would permit. This return was founded on a principle that M<sup>r</sup> Dunscomb who settled with the Virginia Line had charged to the account on which the Officers must receive certificates, the Specie pay of 1782 and 1783 whether it was actually received by him or not and that every advance had also been deducted but having since I made that return revised the accounts on which the settlements of the line were actually made and entered them in the Books of This Office — I find on a careful review that the ballances in specie differ very considerably when ascertained from them settlements — I have therefore made out a return founded on principles which will do justice both to the public and the individuals and which will shew the real sum to be paid to the line — and also a return which will shew the sums which the Officers have received beyond the pay that was due to them, and which ought to be refunded until further demands for subsistence are sufficient to cover them.

Honorable Secretary at War        Sam<sup>l</sup> L. Pierce

38426:
Philadelphia Dec<sup>r</sup> 20. 1786

My Worthy Friend

The long Friendship that has subsisted between us Emboldens me to trouble you at this time the more especially as I suspect it will come immediately in your department as the state of the Public Accounts when I would wish you to trouble yourself in with regard to my accounts with the Public in recording my Company from settling my deficiency of Wages which was fixed up to one till the 1<sup>st</sup> of August 1779. I resigned my Commission in April 1780 the most of my Papers was taken from me by the British at the Battle of Brandywine but my Muster Rolls I expect can be found in the Public Office and as there must be a very considerable balance in my favor. I would take it as a very particular obligation in you to trouble yourself in my favor and should it ever lay in my way I will amply repay the obligation, your answer to this with instructions how I shall proceed will much oblige Yours to Frien<sup>d</sup> &c

Joseph Howell Esq<sup>r</sup>           Gibbs Jones

[A.R. If you could send me an Account of the Cash I received I would take it as a favor as I can't tell the exact sum — pleased to direct for me at M<sup>r</sup>s Bittertons, &c. &c.]

38429:
New York, December 22<sup>d</sup> 1796

[From T?]

I have received your letter of the 1<sup>st</sup> of [Septem?] last respecting the pay of the three companies of your Line, which appears to be claimed by John Merrell Esquire — when I was in Georgia the Auditor of the State, produced to me the pay rolls of these companies, which it appeared he had registered and had deliver the State securities thereon into the hands of Lieut. Van Waggonen for him to deliver to the Soldiers. I had then no objection to the price exhibited for the performance of the services of these men but it was my opinion that the State ought Prior to the same being pased to call Mr. Waggonen to account, and exhibit to me the receipts or discharges of the money thereon that the State ought not to have a credit beyond its payment and that the discharges may be lodged as a security against another claim from the Soldiers. I supposed that the claim of Mr. Merrell was a subsequent transaction, in which he had made a purchase of these certificates from the Solders, a transaction with which the United States had no concern In this opinion I still continue, and I suppose myself not authorised to consider the pay of these men as due to any individual, but on the contrary assumed by the State and therefore according to the Resolves of Congress to be

Dear Sir

Captain Matthew Scott late of the 13<sup>th</sup> Regiment Pennysl<sup>a</sup> has applied for a Settlement of his Accounts the Claim being founded in this Office I have made a Stating of the Account in order to inform you that he is not indebted to the United States the balance now appearing due cannot be granted without a Special Resolution of Congress —— On the 5<sup>th</sup> August 1779 Captain Scott made a Settlement with the late Auditors of the Army and received a provision of eight dollars for imbursing the sum his departure immediately from Camp prevented the Mistake being rectified at that time and the Account being now mustered lays out of my power to do it you will therefore be pleased on Settling with him to Credit the difference between W<sup>t</sup> allowed & the B secured to the United States — Captain Scott informs me he has the Inlistments at Philadelphia which will enable you to form the Settlement — Cap<sup>t</sup> Scott has a credit of 420 dollars being so much returned unto the Pay Office as part of Spunning Pay which sum still remains in the P.M. J Howell Dep<sup>r</sup>

Sir.     Burlington County New Jersey Dec. 30<sup>th</sup> 1786

In Consequence of your letter to M<sup>r</sup> Symms one of our late Delegates in Congress with your reasonings upon my Letter to that Gentleman previous thereto — with Complain-ings M<sup>r</sup> Symms was Pleased to Honor me with. —

The Objections you make to my demands, I hope will be removed If you will be so good as to Reconsider the whole matter respecting my accounts — (my vouchers being in your office) You have then stated my accounts. — In the year 1777 I am Charged with 11.600 dollars, the amount of my Vouchers in that year 14.468 ¼ Dollars the Allowance for Recruiting my Regt you say is as follows — the whole of the men that were Raised that year was 619 those Bounties amount to 10.266 Dollars, Continues to the officers 634 Dollars some Incidental Expenses which will probably amount to 556 Dollars. — which makes up the Sum of 11.800 Dollars which I am Charged with. —

The above Statement Seems to Balance the accounts though very unfavourable to me as well as unjust. But whether all the Monthly Rolls for that whole year are in your office or not I cannot Determine, But further think they are not. Also the Number of men that Served Cannot be determined, as Some of the officers were on the recruiting Service nearly the whole year and as we Served In or near our own State, Bounties free — [undecipherable] offered and were never rejected. If a Bounty could be raised in the Regt. —

New York Jan<sup>y</sup> 25 1787.

My dear Sir, Inspector Wadsworth Left evening he informs me that he is very unusually detained untill the 27 or 28<sup>th</sup>. Gen<sup>l</sup> Shephearse took Phelps at the Magazine in Springfield on the 18 with a thousand men in favor of government. The insurgents were mustering in his neighborhood, and had collected 1500, as was considered. An action was expected. Gen<sup>l</sup> Lincoln was to march, from Boston about 14 miles from Boston on the 19 or 21<sup>st</sup> with about 2000 men. he expects to be at Worcester on the 23 to protect the courts of Common pleas. and was to be joined there by 1200 men raised in Worcester County. If the insurgents do not meet him there he is to proceed to Hampshire, and take up the leaders of the party — The post is just closing I will write more on Monday next.

Y<sup>r</sup> Knox

Governour Morris Esq<sup>r</sup>

38513:
War Office February 7<sup>th</sup> 1787

[To]

I have the honor to acknowledge the receipt of your Excellency's two favors of the 21 of November and the 26 January, the latter enclosing a list of the officers appointed to command the quota of New Hampshire.

There having been no Congress for about three months past, those measures than were directed on the 21 of October for the establishment of the troops could not be forwarded by the board of treasury within the approbation of Congress. This circumstance has almost entirely retarded the commencement of the recruiting service excepting in Connecticut and Massachusetts, in which a little money has been supplied for the purpose of recruiting.

There have been proposals for certain ranks for all the objects of the troops. But the terms are of such a nature as to require the decision of Congress thereon. As this honorable body has been but recently organized, the subject has not yet been discussed. It is probable however that their opinion and the result will be known in ten or fifteen days. If

the contracts alluded to should take place, the troops will probably for a certain time be well furnished, if they do not, it will be difficult for the treasury board to fund the ways and means to furnish the pay, clothing, subsistence and transportation of the troops in any tolerable manner.

I shall therefore find myself restrained from giving Major Cass any orders until the event of the proposed contracts is determined.

The arms and accoutrements for Major Cass's corps are in Boston in the care of Colonel Frothm, As soon as suitable provisions can be made for commencing the recruiting service in New Hampshire, they shall be forwarded to Major Cass.

The commissions will be made out presented to the President of Congress for his signature, and when completed, shall be forwarded to your Excellency.

I have the honor to be

With the highest respect

Your Excellency's

Most obedient servant

H Knox

His Excellency
President Sullivan

38530:
[No 95] To the Honble Secretary at War, of the United States.

Pittsfield, Jany 9<sup>th</sup> 1787

Dear Sir.

I was the last evening honored with the receipt of your favor of the 5<sup>th</sup> P<sup>r</sup> Bearer North. —

As the State of Massachusetts has ordered a body of men to be kept up for four months from the 19<sup>th</sup> instant, who will be stationed in the disaffected Counties, to watch the motions, & to counteract the Insurgents, the Magazine will in my opinion be quite safe, as no body of men can at tact to a point unnoticed, therefore we need not now guard against an attack here, in force — To prevent the Magazine of arms from being fired, & the foundation of the powder magazine from being sapped, are the only objects which from now claim your attention; to this service, 10 men will be fully competent. —

You may also be assured that while I retain any command in this part of the country, the security of the Magazine shall be among the first objects of my attention.

The Rebellion is so effectually giving way in every part, that little danger is to be apprehended from it.

Jn<sup>o</sup>

38545:
[Private]      New York 28 February 1787

My Dear Sir  I have presented the board of treasury to give me orders on M<sup>r</sup> Imlay for 2000 dollars which are enclosed to you in my public letter of this day.

In order to facilitate the business I have consented to suffer the warrants to be drawn in my name, and to become accountable with the comptroller for the same. I hope you may be able to negotiate them! The one for the quarter masters department, is drawn for a larger sum than will be required for that department. I have expected that you pay the overplus to my order, as I am in great distress on account of some public debts and the board can afford me no other relief, than that now proposed

If you could negotiate 200 dollars more, than the sums now forwarded, and will inform me so, I will endeavor

to get the board to give the quartermaster General their warrants for that sum, to be applied to the substance of the officers and the pay of the men of Colonel Humphreys's Corps.

Congress have reduced the pay of the civil staff — every officer has been reduced more or less excepting those serving at War. This plan you have low to be the true law

Congress wise for abating taking off the leisure of the troops now pressing — as you and M<sup>r</sup> Morris would not maintain them — It is absolutely they must be reduced — Keep this however entirely a Secret.

I am my dear Sir
Your truly affectionate
H Knox

Col<sup>o</sup> Wadsworth

[No. February 1787]

New York 24 March 1787

I have received my dear friend, your favor of the 9<sup>th</sup> instant concerning a certificate of the insurance Legrand & Philips. With the best of my opinions before him I am unable to do it. The accounts of the regiment are not yet settled, and provision made to pay the balances that have be found due on account of the pay of the last year. Some modification of the troops now in service will soon take place, and I hope that an effectual arrangement may be taken for the payment of the arrearages. The moment the thing is practicable he will be paid. I enclose you the certificate.

There are nine States represented at present. The States generally have chosen for the convention. Rhode Island and Connecticut the only exceptions —

Thanks to a probable circumstance, that Gen<sup>l</sup> Washington now has it is. I hope you will be a Misrepresentation worthy to expire in reframing his God to continue in the chair of Mr. Society.

The little presatory memoirs of the insurgents of Massachusetts, are the last struggle of an expiring faction. In the Supreme Court are with vigor, and a few executions have place the business of insurgency in Massachusetts will most probably be finished for the present.

The State of Massachusetts have voted £5000 lawful for the State's party pay to federal troops in that State. I say voted because but a small part of the £5000 originally voted had been paid. The two sums together amount to £5000 dollars. This served to the purpose, and pay two men, and their officers an considerable time. I have ordered Col<sup>o</sup> Jackson to the

if the State will furnish him with the means to march his corps to Springfield, in which case I found made Col<sup>o</sup> Humphreys someone has men not to be feeble. I don't desire you to assist in any degree on the force but state you pleased be made fede workers.

adieu dear,
Your affectionate

38605:

War Office, March 28<sup>th</sup> 1797

$s   #226

Agreeably to your request of the 8<sup>th</sup> instant, I now enclose you two orders, drawn in my favor on William Imlay Esq<sup>r</sup> by the board of Treasury for 1800 Dollars The order on account of rations is intended to be placed in your hands to defray the expences of rations, and the one for the quarter Masters department to defray the expences on that line. You will please immediately on the receipt of these bills to enclose me your duplicate receipts for them.

You will please to keep the accounts separate and distinct, and the monies drawn on account of the warrants now forwarded cannot be applied to any other objects than those specified without involving me in great embarrassment the comptroller of the treasury.

As you are fully imbursed with the necessity of accurate vouchers for the settlement of public accounts, you will please to direct that yours be kept perfectly so.

I should have been happy that some further advances, could have been made to the troops at this time, on account of their pay and Subsistence, but the board of Treasury, cannot make unconal advances to the troops, and those on the Ohio have not as yet for this year had either pay or Subsistence.

I am Sir
With great Respect
Your very humb<sup>le</sup> Servant

Col<sup>o</sup> Jeremiah Wadsworth                                       Knox

M<sup>r</sup> James Moran has repaired and Cleaned no three rand Stands of Arms which you mention in a Contract with him, in your letter of March 16<sup>th</sup> 1787 — which for which I have given him a Certificate — 320 of the in Arms that was taken in from the Army in the year 1783 & 84 in good order — the Other 480 are to be deducted from the damaged Arms, and add to the good Stands —

By the Gen<sup>ls</sup> previous Stock I know any piece of the Arms repaired, think it would be best to begin with the that want [strikethrough: Sticking fast on from] of 48 as new locks can be fitted to the Stocks — then the old one taken to repare the Other — Also it will take Care to Need to half Stock and Splice the Other which wants it — And at present we have not [strikethrough: that] to fit to repare 320 — then I propose that the public have New Stocks and locks, sufficient for the whole —

I believe there is about 3000 which will be worth finishing, and about 1400 that will be worth —

M<sup>r</sup> Moran is very desirous of doing them, and I believe he will do them as Cheap as any body —

He Says that he will Stock and Clean, the that want it for two Dollars each, and repare and Clean the rest for One dollar each, which will every but little better then M<sup>r</sup> for the whole — And he will make what Buff pans, locks and Stocks it Necessary for the whole, also find his own Cords and Stiles to Compleat them —

The public finds Stocks and all the materials to work With, finds a Mill, Bar, Glue, thread and all the hireing Supplies Such as mentioned above, and what is needful into them Clean Cart rough, and to Splice them up —

He will do them as great a place as you please think proper to have them done

Inclosed is a Certificate of what furniture has been delivered to M<sup>r</sup> Moran [undecipherable] is —

Captain Burbeck has made application for fore more for his Company at the Rate of 16 Cents for Month, for 100 Men, which is Inform me he drew at Boston and Springfield, agreeable to a Resolution of Congress Some time in the year 1786 —

If the public are able I to let me know the allowance for the Troops, in Ammunition and Winter, and I shall be able to have any work pure helpful, I will forward an estimate for that purpose —

Now is a very good time to purchase what work is needful for both Ammunition and Winter, and if your Chief, I will request you to let you know the lowest it Can be purchased for delivered here —

The Hon<sup>ble</sup>

38622:
[Private]

New York 8 April 1787

My dear Sir

I have to acknowledge the receipt of your public and private favors of the 1<sup>st</sup> inst. Your two favors of the 4<sup>th</sup> together with the Solomon, which was received by a vessel yesterday in high perfection, for which M<sup>rs</sup> Knox presents you her thanks.

It is by no means my intention that the troops should be deprived of the shoes and shirts you were preparing for them. On the contrary, I pray you to furnish them, and you shall receive your payments out of the monies already in your possession or otherwise at your option, only let me have a list of all the clothing you have furnished.

The troops will perhaps be to be uniformly clothed — would it be agreeable to you to undertake the supply from the beginning on the principles of receiving warrants to the amount on the receiver of Connecticut?

I will endeavor to have your arrears filled to your satisfaction, necessarily but which shall have hundreds thousands under which at the means there be arranged.

If Colonel Humphreys has arrived, he will be able to inform you that Congress have under consideration the subject of the troops — nothing however is yet decided. By Humphreys post, I shall be more explicit.

Thank you cordially for the information respecting the damaged powder, although I am strongly inclined to believe that there is some capital error in it — Cap<sup>t</sup> Price who has the charge of the stores at West point, is a man in whom I confide most unboundedly. He has no authority to sell any powder. I beg you therefore my dear sir to require minutely of your informant, whom, where, and of whom the powder was bought, that I may minutely investigate the affair — If true which I flatter myself is impossible, a rigorous procedure must

instantly take place — but I must have proofs before I begin.

I have seen advertisements [cancelled] for the sale of damaged powder at Brooklime[?] and ferry, ten or fifteen days ago. I will enquire to whom it belonged, to whom sold, & the price — most probably this is the powder of which you have been informed — Pray give early attention to this matter.

I am your affectionate

H Knox

Col<sup>o</sup> Wadsworth

Agreeably to the orders of Congress I have this day directed Col<sup>l</sup> Jackson to disband all the troops rais'd agreeably to the orders of Congress of the 20<sup>th</sup> of October 1786 excepting the two companies of Artillery commanded by you and Cap<sup>t</sup> Savage.

I have requested him to discharge any defective men that may be in those two companies of Artillery — to have them uniformly clothed, and marched to Springfield as soon as possible.

You will therefore apply to him immediately for the accomplishment of those objects, and also for one months pay, and the subsistence of your Officers to the 1<sup>st</sup> of May. You will therefore now set for to march to Springfield with all possible dispatch.

You will apply to M<sup>r</sup> Stephen Bruce for four Waggons or four Ox teams to transport the baggage to Springfield, and also to supply you with fuel, and any little contingent expences of the march.

When you arrive at Springfield you will find an arrangement for furnishing you with provisions laid from be. You will apply to Colonel Jackson for Arms and Accoutrements, and a supply of Barrack utensils.

You are to be stationed at Springfield for the sole purpose of protecting the federal property at that place. You will maintain the highest discipline in the troops under your command, and punish them Severely for any injury to the persons or properties of the Inhabitants.

You will report to me your proceedings in consequence of these instructions, and inform me of the time when you shall march.

Should your clothing be on hand and not finished you must not be detained on that account for any length of time — But you will make arrangements with M<sup>r</sup> Bruce for forwarding of it to you at Springfield, taking with you Arms and other indispensible articles.

I address you these orders on the principle of your being Senior to Captain Savage who is to regard these orders from you as if coming from me to him personally.

I am Sir
Your very Humble Servant
H Knox

Captain Henry Burbeck.

War Office, 14 April 1787.

Sir,

I take the liberty to enclose to you a letter for Colonel Humphreys, as it contains warrants on the loan offices of Connecticut amounting to 1275 dollars for the pay and subsistence of the troops — The sooner it is presented, the better — There will be a further sum due, on account of pay, but it cannot now be precisely ascertained, for want of the muster rolls. I have directed Colonel Humphreys to have the men mustered without delay, and the muster rolls forwarded to me, in order that a statement of the pay due may be made out, and warrants to issue for the same. I have made this arrangement with the treasury board.

In the formation of your accounts you will please to arrange all articles of clothing in one account, medicine in another, fuel, straw, transportation, hire of barracks, utensils, tools, and all the supplies not comprehended in either of the foregoing or subsequent accounts, into the Quarter Master's department. The rations will of course be by themselves, in one receipt, signed by the commanding officer. The colonel or paymaster will arrange the account of pay.

I am,
with great respect,
Sir
Your most obed: humble Serv:
H Knox

Colonel Jeremiah Wadsworth.

Sir

In consequence of your favor of the 18th instant, I have obtained two warrants from the Board of Treasury, on William Sunday Esq<sup>r</sup> receiver of Continental taxes, for the State of Connecticut; one for 3000, and the other for 1300 dollars, which are to discharge the arrearages to Colonel Humphreys's corps, and for other public purposes, for the payment of both of which, I beg your influence. Encouraged by your kind offer, I have endorsed those warrants, payable to you, and now enclose them; and request you to be so good as to forward me, immediately, duplicate receipts of the tenor herein enclosed for the same. You will also please to inform me, when you shall receive the money for the same, which you will please to hold subject to my order.

As soon as Colonel Humphreys shall forward the musters, and accounts, a warrant will be issued, by me, on the paymaster, for the precise sum which shall be found due.

I have the honor to be
Sir
Your most obedient, and
very humble Serv<sup>t</sup>

For                    Philadelphia April 28. 1787.

Discoursing with Cap James M<sup>c</sup>lean Concerning the gratuity of the year 1779. which I am deficient in he advised me to apply to your honor as the only person who could see me righted therein. or direct me where to apply for it. you will please to examine the Books and furnish me with such certificates as may be necessary for the recovery thereof. I formerly belonged to the 5<sup>t</sup> Penn<sup>a</sup> Regt Cap James Moorer Company Commanded by Colonel Johnson; and from thence transferred to the invalid Reg<sup>t</sup> the year 1777. if your honor will please to take notice of this poor epistle. and write to Philadelphia you will please to direct to M<sup>rs</sup> Gavon Hamilton, Tobacconist in Second Street as he is a man of Some note. and will be pleased to excuse this trouble from your Hum: Ser<sup>t</sup>.

W<sup>m</sup> M<sup>c</sup>Dougall

C Major Howell

I do myself the Honor to enclose you a Statement of the pay an clothing due that part of Colonel Humphreys' Regiment raised in Connecticut — the muster Rolls of Cap. Throop's Company is imperfect in respect to Joseph Hill and Bejamin Tooley, whose dates of enlistment are not inserted. Office of army accounts
New York May 5. 1787.

Dear Sir

The Certificate you allude to in your letter of the 2. Instant was issued to Alexander Fowler Esquire (late Auditor for the Western district) in consequence of Sundry goods supplied a detachment of the Maryland Troops under the Command of Major Marbury at Fort Pitt in the year 1783. M<sup>r</sup> Fowler expected to have received the cash for those Supplies, but being disappointed there was no other method left to secure him than by transmitting the charges to the Commissioner for the State of Maryland and having the same deducted from the pay of the men — this was done and the sum mentioned in the Certificate was the amount So Stopped and passed to M<sup>r</sup> Fowlers Credit in the books of this Office —

It may be necessary to mention also that M<sup>r</sup> Fowler assigned the debt to Casper Singer and Sons of your deity to whom the Certificate was given

Sam<sup>l</sup> & Jos. Howell J<sup>nr</sup>

John Nicholson Esq<sup>t</sup>

38687:
Dear Sir

New York, May 7 1787

Agreeably to the request contained in your letter of the 23 ulti[received on saturday last] I have sent on the papers relative to the accounts of Colonel Baylor — Doctor Bond's amounts have been Settled by M<sup>r</sup> Walker, and the balance of his pay to the 31<sup>st</sup> December 1783 has also been granted him by me, the Doctor Claims an extention of pay to the 1 January 1786. which I do not think prudent to grant without your approbation, altho I am of opinion that he is in some measure entitled to it, and appears that he received money of the late Superintendent of Finance for his department as late as august 1783, and paid his Clerk to the 1<sup>st</sup> November following, the Doctor has wrote you on the Subject and sent Copies of Certificates given him by Colonel Walker and M<sup>r</sup> Hillegas, by which you will be able to Judge of the propriety of his claim. if you are of opinion that his pay should be extended you will please to inform me as soon as possible. General Putnam has made application for the pay retained and that which was granted by the States in 1780, also for his Rations for the year 1780. and 1781 — I want to Congress your report relative to the pay, the Rations was not mentioned as you have indorsed your opinion that he was entitled to the rations from which I supposed you meant to grant them, was this your intention? if you let me know I suppose a Certificate from the General or from the Commissary that rations were not drawn during this period will be Necessary previous to any allowance —

Have you received my letter of the 19 April inclosing one for M<sup>r</sup> Duncomb with Certificates for him

General —

Deerfield 1 May 1797

Sir.

Meeting with an opportunity by Cap<sup>t</sup> Norris, I take the Liberty again to solicit the Copies of my Voucher Accounts that I wrote to you in last Summer and again in February last but have not Recd any answer. M<sup>r</sup> Snowman tells me that you did not fully understand my meaning — the Certifycate that I want of you is that the Accounts of N<sup>o</sup> 4 2. 3 &4 Contains a full Statement of all General States Military Accounts; and that he has legally Accounted for all the Moneys that he Received from the Publick while an Officer in the American Army — this Certificate I wish you to sign Officially and transmit to me by the return of Cap<sup>t</sup> Norris who will have the Honour of delivering them.

This Gentleman is the Father of one Eliphalet Norris who was taken up some time since for Passing Counterfeit Publick Securities. his Business is to See what is become of his Son, altho the Son may have been Guilty of faults, yet
38693:
[Letter from the Secretary of War of 9 May 97 Enclosing a Return of the Military Stores &Munitions]

War Office May 9 1797

Sir,

Your letter of the 23 ultimo acknowledging the receipt of the public arms and accoutrements from Col<sup>n</sup> Williams Allen has come to hand.

I now enclose to you a copy of a return of military stores the property of the United States deposited on Rhode Island under the care of a M<sup>r</sup> Elnah Flagg, and an order for you to receive them from him. In the transacting this buisness I have to request that you will take the trouble to have a return of all the articles which you may receive from M<sup>r</sup> Flagg and signed by him with your receipt. Parole made out and forwarded to this office. As there has never been any order for the delivery of any part of these stores it is to be expected that all the articles specified in the within return are to be found in M<sup>r</sup> Flagg's care, but in case of any alterations you will please to have the same noted, toether with the reasons for the same, and I afterwards can readily inform me accurately of the real state and condition of the stores which are to be placed on your care at the risk of the public and all necessary expenses

of storage collection and removal to be reimbursed to you on your furnishing an account thereof supported by full and sufficient vouchers

I am Sir

With real esteem

Your most obedient

humble Servant

H Knox

Col<sup>o</sup> Jeremiah Olney
38694:
Office of army accounts
New York May 9<sup>th</sup> 1787

Dear Sir

Your letter (bearing no date) enclosing power of attorney from Pierce Manning, and Captain William Mackay, was received this day, herewith you will receive a Certificate N<sup>o</sup> 9,277,2 for 90 <sup>90</sup>/100 Dollars due the former — the claim of the latter cannot at present be admitted unless by a special act of Congress — as all army Claims are foreclosed by an act of that honorable body, if not presented previous to the 1. August 1786. —

I have examined the returns of prisoners filed in this Office, and cannot find Captain Mackay among any of them, or any notice taken of him in the several exchanges that took place, from which I have every reason to suppose that he had been forgot or was not Considered as a prisoner by the British Commissary — this was the case with Captain Potts of the 5<sup>th</sup> Regiment who was wounded and taken prisoner the same day, and as a General exchange of prisoners took place in October 1780, particularly with those who were taken in 1778 & 1777 I am of opinion that Captain Mackay must consider himself of the number and be satisfied with his being Continued and paid to the 1 January 1781 — Cap Mackay was much more fortunate than many others in a similar Situation who were under the necessity of retiring with a years pay and without any other emolument — however

my
38695:
[130]

my opinion need not prevent him from making an application to Congress if he thinks himself injured in a settlement —

Geo. Rich. Hampton                           Jos. Howell J<sup>r</sup>

Dear Sir              New York May 10. 1787.

Your favor of the 30<sup>th</sup> ultimo with its enclosures was received the 8<sup>th</sup> Instant The one for M<sup>r</sup> O was delivered agreably to your request. a curious claim has been made by a M<sup>r</sup> John Wait of Boston, for the pay due sundry directors of the Massachusetts line, which it seems he purchased at Camp, and from the circumstance of their not having received until late in the year 1785 or at least until after the treaty of peace, he urges the propriety and justice of his claim and has interested M<sup>r</sup> Gotham in favor of his application which is now before the Board of Treasury — I have examined your instructions from M<sup>r</sup> Morris's, and do not find any clause in them that relates to this business. I therefore suppose you have been governed by some standing resolution of Congress respecting forbearance by direction, which I do not recollect when it was passed. I have not countenanced in the least this precedent, as it would open a door for a multiplicity of applications, and involve this office in a Labyrinth of business, not to serve the public but only to answer the views of Speculating traders. —

The ordinance requiring the five Commissioners to be appointed, passed in Congress yesterday —

Pittsburg May 16 1787

Sir

I was favoured last week with a Letter from Cap<sup>t</sup> Joseph Henricks in which is stated a large Account against me, taken from the Publick Books, the Information Greatly Surprises me, as having no Idea of any such amount existing. In Order to have it speedily Settled and Cleared I have taken the Liberty of Inciting under Cover to you, an Answer with such Information that will I hope enable him fully to Settle the same — which Please thank you to deliver to him, and excuse the Freedom of Sir —

Your &<sup>c</sup>
John Pierandpss —        Hugh Bayard

Pittsburg May 16 1787

Dear Sir

I Received your favour by Isaac Schuyler Dated at New York Feb 7<sup>th</sup> 1787, the Contents of which Greatly Surprises me because I have no Idea that such a large sum could possibly stand against me this time of day. I am happy however that I write to you, and that you have favoured me with the account as it now Stands, which may prevent a great deal of trouble in future. — When I was Ordered to the Westward

In consequence of M<sup>r</sup> Pierce being called to the Southward to transact some public business, the settlement of the army accounts during his absence devolves on me; I have therefore to acknowledge the receipt of your favor to M<sup>r</sup> Pierce of the 8<sup>th</sup> Instant, and agreeably to your request have enclosed you a Statement of the accounts of the late Colonel Dunbar, which will inform you the reason of M<sup>r</sup> Pierce's request to the pay Table to stop the issue of their State notes to you.— When Captain Dunbar received the certificates for the pay retained from the Colonel, we were not possessed of the two last charges, but which have since been forwarded by the pay table at Hartford— those charges as you will observe, brings the Colonel greatly indebted to the United States.— I cannot for my own part suppose, that as the sum of 5200 dollars was received for the express purpose of "paying the Continental Bounties" that the Colonel has either given this money to some one or more persons to distribute it, or that he did himself, and has neglected to take receipts. My supposition arises from a knowledge which I have of the Settlements made by the Officers of the Connecticut Line— and that few of them on a final close of their accounts— became indebted to the United States— perhaps you may be enabled to throw further
