39326:
New York June 9<sup>th</sup> 1788

Dear Sir

Your favor of the 21<sup>st</sup> ultimo is duly received and when the present present arrives you may expect my thanks —

M<sup>r</sup> Hardy the accomptant informs me that the State of Virginia is having settle with a number of her citizens for supplies furnished Services performed the returns of which have not the been made to the commissioners of the Treasury, they have no claim on the account now presented which circumstance prevents them from settling any claims from your quarter of the nature presented, as the period for receiving all claims on the United States is fast approaching I am of opinion that the certificate transmitted had better remain with the accomptant for if they are withdrawn the proprietors may be embarrassed a settlement — I shall therefore retain them until you please to order the contrary

Your Convention I understand are now in session & at two papers cost but a trifle I will particularly thank you for one that contains the debates which you can just under cover to M<sup>r</sup> Pierce, in return you shall receive one from me when the Gentlemen of this State convene which will be the 17<sup>th</sup> current — The anti federalists have a large majority here in Convention

I feel exceeding am happy to inform you that M<sup>r</sup> Pierce is in a very poor state of Health, his [undecipherable] convince him in a deep consumption & in my opinion nothing but a miracle can preserve him — That cursed business in Virginia I presume has been the cause — he has for some time retired from all business —

Doct<sup>r</sup> Goulds papers are received

I am & Yours
Jas Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>.

Andrews Dunscomb Esq<sup>r</sup>

New York 11 June 1788 [Private]

My dear Sir

You ask in your letter what I have considered as a probable issue, respecting the line of conduct that will probably be pursued by this State. In a word the antifederal interest is so powerful as to give them a majority of 45 or 46 out of 66. They are obstinate and artful they will not probably have the hardihood to openly reject the constitution Should Virginia adopt it, but they will adjourn to a distant day. They will in the mean time consult on the conduct which will best promote their policy. Every thing rests on the decision of Virginia — if she will adopt the constitution all things will be easy notwithstanding the crooked policy of this State and [undecipherable]

To these symptoms may be able to frustrate the constant [undecipherable] their plans and [undecipherable] — Independency dependant they may prove the [undecipherable] dependant convince & their interest

I am &c
Your friend & humble
[undecipherable]
servant [undecipherable]

To O. H<sup>o</sup> Williams
39333:
New York 13 June 1788

My dear Sir

I received your favor of the 6<sup>th</sup> instant and its enclosure by the post last evening and it will be forwarded to M<sup>r</sup> Goth to morrow so that he will receive it on the same day. He resides at Brooston, about a miles east of Morris Town the same place formerly occupied by M<sup>r</sup> Sam<sup>d</sup> Ogden.

The proposed constitution seems to be as well received as could have been conjectured by the most sanguine. The opposition is now complete in itself and subject to a thousand misrepresentations. It is not surprising that it has had its enemies. Mankind are infinitely diversified in their situations and faculties, and being constrained by local circumstances to view the same objects through different mediums and with different apparatuses it is no wonder that their conceptions and opinions should be different. Happy indeed is it for the public and I hope for posterity that so many states have already agreed to it and that none have rejected excepting Rhode Island. The conduct of the majority of that State — The insurrections of Masscachusetts and the opposition to the impost by New York have been the corrective means of rousing america to an attention to her liberties — Thank Heaven a government is projected which in case of a storm will shelter the principles of liberty and its citizens from the rude attacks of anarchy and tyranny.

Eight States have already adopted the Constitution, and there is good reason to believe that Virginia will also receive it on the plan of Masscachusetts — You may cherish the hope dear friend that by the 4<sup>th</sup> of July you will receive the information that Virginia is the 9<sup>th</sup> State which has ratified the Constitution.

How far Virginia's acceptance will influence the conduct of this State is uncertain as the antifederalists have a decided Majority of forty or 50. Their present policy appears to be to postpone in a dilatory way. This will be more decisive than a rejection, and they mean to have that operation — In every event however a keen conduct they will find 2/3 of the State as ready to oppose them as him from intrinsic [undecipherable: probable reading loftiness]. The minorities of the other States will be to support them.

The case of New Hampshire seems to be concealed — we cannot learn any thing decisive [undecipherable] the interior. Should they reject the constitution, the would also blind indeed! North Carolina will follow Virginia — Permits me my dear friend to congratulate you on this affection manifested for you by your fellow Citizens on your late election to the office of Lieut Governor — Mrs Knox presents her love to you, and I hope to take you by the hand in Boston in about 3 weeks hence

In the mean time
I am your truly
Sincere friend Knox

[Gov. Lincoln]

[marginalia, rotated: Knox / June 13 1788]

39337:
Extract of a letter from Brigadier Harmon dated Fort Harmar June 15, 1788.

I had the honor of addressing you last on the 26<sup>th</sup> of April, form Fort Pitt since whict I have to acknowledge the receipt of your letters of the 11<sup>th</sup> and 21<sup>st</sup> April. —

On the 27<sup>th</sup> April I left Fort Pitt in the Barge, and after six days extreme hard labor in ascending the rapid river Allegheny arrived at Captain Hbarts works on French Creek.

Captain Hbart with his small command has done an immense deal of work there. The Garrison was found to be in excellent order. There are a number of Shawnee and Muncy tribes continually in his neighbourhood who conduct themselves very peaceably. Indeed I know of no officer who manages the Indians better than Captain Hbart.

The Senecas in particular place great confidence in him, he is a great favorite among them.
39339:
New York June 1<sup>th</sup> 1788

Dear Sir

Capt Cushing having been unfortunate in his application to Congress relative to his claim for rank he has me exceedingly anxious for his future prospects therefore wishes to engage in the office for some time in order to extricate himself from the difficulties he now lies under— You may recollect that I sometime since mentioned to you the necessity of printing the printed Registers & transmitting the errors to the Several loan offices— This business I am exceedingly anxious should be completed and if it meets your approbation could wish that Capt Cushing might be employed on that business.

Pray do you wish to have the whole of your cash account drawn out & the vouchers examined? or from what period would you wish them to commence? I have requested Mr Audibrets attention to this business— at present Mr Audibrets illness prevents his presence at the office

I hope to have the pleasure of seeing you tomorrow if the weather will permit my taking my child an airing—

Joseph Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>.

John Pierce Esquire

39351:
War Office, June 23, 1788

Sir,

I have the honor to inform you of your being appointed Agent for the Cherokee Nation of Indians, agreeably to the enclosed resolve of Congress of the 19<sup>th</sup> of June 1788.

I also enclose you the ordinance of Congress passed on the 7th August 1786 for the regulation of the Indian department.

You will observe by your appointment that you are to act in subordination to the Superintendent with whom you will constantly communicate respecting your proceedings.

I beg leave to observe that the affairs of the United States are so circumstanced as to render the most rigid oeconomy necessary in all their transactions, and that no expences for presents in [strikethrough: subsisting] subsisting the Indians will be allowed unless by the special direction of Congress.

[found by an advertisement] that the stage will come through to this place from New Haven after the 1<sup>st</sup> of July — If Bate or any other person in whom you can repose full confidence comes in the stage forward the money by that conveyance, unless you will be here yourself by the 10<sup>th</sup> If so take it under your own charge — please to let me know the mode and any rate suffer me to rely either on your sending it safely or bringing it yourself — but not

I rejoice with you on New Hampshires adoption — It secures the point — Virginia will accept without doubt with a more Majority Maddison writes so by the last Post dated last

But it is not certain whether the question would have been taken on the 21, or that a small adjournment of a few days should then take place to accomodate the Assembly which was so much on the 23 — a majority was supposed to be secured in either case — but if the [undecipherable] adjournment took place until tomorrow as was probable the majority will be encreased by the news from New Hampshire for this will reach Richmond this day or early tomorrow morning —

I admire your curses — but you have no occasion to use

use them — The Antis will have the occasion —

I know not how it goes in the convention of this State — the Minority it is said has encreased to 25, whether it will rise higher is uncertain — If my information be true they will propose certain amendments and then adjourn The influence of New Hampshire & even Virginia would not be conclusive it might move some — but the Majority would be inflexible

I shall not be here after the 8<sup>th</sup> or 10<sup>th</sup>

I am y<sup>r</sup> sincere & affectionate

M<sup>r</sup> Pierce is .....

As<sup>t</sup> Com<sup>r</sup> of Army Ac<sup>ts</sup>

New York June 30<sup>th</sup> 1782.

Dear Sir

Your favor of the 24<sup>th</sup> Inst was not handed me
before this morning — I will endeavor to have a copy of the account of
Col Wood prepared for you by tomorrow post — It will not be in my
power to close them without being in possession of Col Bayards voucher —
Col Wells certainly must have the vouchers for account current
or how could he have settled with Col Bayard — If the accounts are
closed without the monies rec<sup>d</sup> by Col Bayards the United States
will be deprived of the means of recovering any part of it —

I have no Muster Roll of the 1<sup>st</sup> Regiment so early as
the month of May 1777. I have therefore applied to the accounts of
M<sup>r</sup> [undecipherable] Dungan late paymaster to that Regiment & find that
Cap<sup>t</sup> Hannaniah Lincoln as attorney has received the pay of Cap<sup>t</sup>
Peter Withington to the 1<sup>st</sup> May 1777 but no remark is made, how or
in what manner he left the service — I am very anxious to inform you
that M<sup>r</sup> Pierce is exceedingly ill & in all probability will not live many
weeks —

I am &c Yours
Joseph h Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>

J<sup>on</sup> Nicholson Esq<sup>a</sup>

New York July 2<sup>d</sup> [17—]

Dear Sir

I wrote you a few lines yesterday by M<sup>r</sup> John Davis and promised to forward by this days post a copy of the accounts of Col. Wood. I was however disappointed having other business to attend to which prevented me from performing my promise.

I now enclose a statement of the accounts formed from the vouchers transmitted me by which you will find that a large balance appears due the United States — exclusive of which several sums of money have been advanced to officers who have long since left the Service I earnest at this day be called on by the United States for settlement — The mode adopted by M<sup>r</sup> Pierce in the settlement of accounts similar to those of Col. Wood has been not to admit any monies advanced unless there were discharges made from the person so receiving for the commanding officers of Regiments were early ordered by Congress to settle the accounts of their recruiting officers and in 1778 the late Commander in Chief issued his orders to the
39378:
July 2<sup>d</sup> 1788.

[underline: Additional Instructions to the Governor of the Territory of the United States North West of the River Ohio, relative to the Treaty to be held with the Western Indians in pursuance of the resolutions of Congress, passed in October last.]

Sir,

An additional sum of twenty thousands dollars has been appropriated for the purposes of procuring a permanent peace with the Indians tribes, with which you are authorized to hold a treaty. This sum and six thousands dollars out of the fourteen thousand heretofore appropriated for holding the said treaty are particularly devoted to be applied solely to the purpose of obtaining a boundary advantageous to the United States, between them & the said Indian tribes, and for further extinguishing by purchase Indian titles [undecipherable: as far as it] can be done on terms beneficial to the Union.

But it is not expected that any further purchases of lands will be made unless on terms evidently advantageous to the United States, or that any parts of the said additional sum will be expended, but in cases apparently necessary.

In fixing a boundary between the United States with the Indian tribes, instead of the East and West line mentioned in your Instructions, you will endeavour to establish an East and West line as far North as the completion of the forty first degree of North Latitude.

In your negotiations with the Indians, you will make immediate payments so far as you shall have monies in hand; but in case you shall find it necessary to engage any considerable part of the said Additional sums, you are to stipulate, that the payments thereof be made in two or three equal annual instalments, the first to be as late in the year 1789 as can be obtained.

Chas Thomson. Sec<sup>y</sup>

the memorialist on the 4<sup>th</sup> of July 1780 put into his hands one hundred & fifty thousand Dollars for the use of the troops under the command of the memorialist which from agreably to the memoirs appears to have been expended for supplies & not for the pay of the troops and as the memorialist prays to be reimbursed in specie for the value of the said sum according to the scale of depreciation. Your assistant commissioner therefore humbly conceives that this part of the claim of the memorialist does not come within the cognizance of this office but that it lies with your Hon<sup>ble</sup> Board to determine the propriety of admitting or rejecting the same —

That on the 26<sup>th</sup> June 1780, the memorialist advanced to Lieut James Gunn 23000 Dollars for recruiting the first Regiment of dragoons for which no receipt appears; this from the Commission of Army accounts rejited as a credit to the memorialist, supposing Lieut Gunns account not to be precluded from a settlement he not having lodged them within the time limited by Congres. This objection is now removed and his name inserted in a return of the claims of sundry officers entitled to pay &c. whose claims were forwarded to the assistant commissioner in Virginia subsequent to the term affixed by the commissioners for the continuance of his office, but by him transmitted to this office prior to the expiration of the time prescribed by Congress. — Your assistant commissioner is therefore of opinion that a certificate may be issued in favor of the memorialist for the specie value of this money &c. charges so litigated against M<sup>r</sup> Gunn when demands against the United States far exceed the sum advanced

[undecipherable] is humbly submitted
Joseph Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>.

The state of New York, having considered this regiment as a part of their Quotas of their state, have generally (if not entirely) settled with the individuals & issued their certificates for pay from the 1<sup>st</sup> of August 1780 to the 1<sup>st</sup> Jan<sup>y</sup> 1782. — If serjeant Dayton has not received his pay from the 1<sup>st</sup> of August 1780 to the day of his discharge I am of opinion he is entitled to it from the state of New York

Joseph Howell Jun<sup>r</sup>

Kentucky May 4<sup>th</sup> 1793

Dear Sir

Permit me to intrude upon your time for a moment and to claim an explanation of the affair to which the inclosed paper refers — Major Brogham alleges that there must be some mistake in your office, as he positively denies having received the [sum] mentioned in the inclosed receipt — In this situation I can only appeal to you for information — If the Major has received the money he does injustice, if not injustice is done him — in either case I am persuaded you will take pleasure to redress the injured party

I must therefore as I am interested in the claim, by the favor of you to transmit me as soon as may be, a transcript from your books formally & officially attested, fully explanatory of this transaction — I send this letter to the Richmond & if it finds you there must beg you to commit your answer

Richmond July 10<sup>th</sup> 1788.

Dear Howell

I have to request the favor of you to shew the Board of Treasury my letters of the 24<sup>th</sup> of January & 2<sup>d</sup> of April last with that of M<sup>r</sup> Pierce of the 27<sup>th</sup> of July in answer to the first in order to read more fully harp the cause of not appearing to have rightly understood the ordinance however it may differ in opinion —

The first advy you will find one at the instance of the three, the second I think speaks a little upon my construction of the ordinance & was meant fully to examine the reasoning & consequences of the opinions given in M<sup>r</sup> Pierce's letter of the 27<sup>th</sup> of July —

There is another matter you may mention that the proof required by the Committ: is mistaken — I called them proofs in full Sterling, and such as might be demanded — & not that they were demanded by the district commish: whose business was only to receive & forwards —

You will take the first proper time to hint these things to their Honors that they may not continue [underline: to] a prove full grounds for fees and the expediency & propriety of urging an extention of time, credit is now fully & Satisfactorily named and admitted — It was always my opinion that the States could bring forth testimony during the term of all the Boards, reserving to the general board a few months to arrange for final [strikethrough: Sitteneconst] determination —

Nothing new at present — pray let me know how our friend Pierce is — Give him my best respects & wishes — What is to be done with the Master Roll? let me know by first post — I am in hast

With usual Friendship
Your most Ob<sup>t</sup>

Jos: Howell Jun<sup>r</sup> Esq<sup>r</sup>           And<sup>w</sup> Dunscomb

Philadelphia May 20, 1798

Respected Friend
Joseph Howell

These are to desire the kind favor the to call on one — Burrell of Fredericksburg who is a justice & stationed in settling the public accounts, & know of him if he has the books of John Mitchell late 22<sup>d</sup> Reg, & if he has on liquidated Certificate in that department, as I unfortunately lost one, a copy of a check I now enclose for thy better information as it belongs to a poor man who has put it into my hands for sale, I would be glad to assist him in the recovery of, or securing the same thy kind service would be obliging to the man & shall be deem'd a great favor done thy friend

Thomas Shute

N.B.— The man is very illiterate & I believe has evil council & thinks he can make me pay it — I pity his ignorance & would do what I can to save him

I have given him the original certificate from J. Mitchell of the inclosed copy

Joseph Howell esq<sup>r</sup>
39422:
[Letter from the Secretary at War 19<sup>th</sup> July 1788]

War Office, July 19<sup>th</sup> 1788.

Sir,

It was with extreme pain I read a letter from Major Allen informing me of his having delivered out a number of articles of military stores on the written application of a number of Gentlemen of Providence.

I have written to him, and I now make the request to you, that you would both exert yourselves to have every article of the said stores returned, or others of equal value.

I have no authority to issue a single article but according to the appropriations of Congress.

I Shall be at Providence, probably in ten days from this date, when I hope to learn that the Gentlemen who influenced Major Allen to make the issue have fully complied with their proposals and to replace the articles in all respects.

I flatter myself that it is unnecessary to give you any instructions against issuing any articles to any person whatever, unless by my orders, or the orders [strikethrough: from] of — the Congress.

I am
Sir.
Your most obedient &
very humble Serv:
H Knox

Colonel Jeremiah Olney,
39423:
[Mr. Oliver's health — I am extremely anxious to hear from
accounts of him.

am Sir &c

Jeph Elwell Esq<sup>r</sup>                    Ja<sup>s</sup> Hopkins]

Portland Cnesday July 19<sup>th</sup> 1799

Sir

I have now an Opportunity Esquire Fletcher
a Member of Congress to write you on the following subject
which I hope to be favored with your answer by the return
of the same Gentleman unless you have a good opportunity
before-time. I should be glad to be informed if there is an
Absolute Monopoly of any coming to your Office at New York
in Philadelphia to make a settlement for the last three
Months payment in Scrip recieved for the Officers and
Soldiers of the late E. Massachusetts Regiment commanded
by Lt Col Commandant S. Sewall, on the Months of
Feb<sup>y</sup> March & April 1799 — if you recollect most of the Soldiers
who had enlisted for the War went home, previous to that
payment being recieved, also many of the Officers, Consequently the Money did, was recieved and was nor could
not be issued in Camp, and was thought by yourself &
several of the Agents that it would be for the Officers &
Soldiers Benefit who Wish to serve with as little inconvenience to them as was possible, to advertise in our
own home, the place and time in the public News-
papers, where they might apply for said wages together
with the Army Toles &c due them, and make the settlement with you at some future time, and not receive
our Commutation untill such Settlement was made,

New York July 25<sup>th</sup> 1788

Sir

M<sup>r</sup> Francis Dension in his Letter of this date informs me that you are employed by John Macgyillasten and John Whitman, John Inglis & Abraham Sianna to receive the Certificates for their claims on the United States which were laid by me before the Comptrolant for liquidation. I have frequently made Application and endeavoured by every possible means to have the claims of these Gentlemen finally adjusted, and feel myself unhappy that my endeavours have been fruitless the Comptrolant informs me that those claims are of so general a Nature that at present they cannot be admitted, but that he supposes some mode will be adopted at a future day to satisfy the Citizens of the United States for the supplies rendered by them, one principal objection to these claims, I find, is that the United States had not a party in the Appraisement of the Articles, and untill a person is appointed for this purpose I am of opinion that claims of a similar nature will not be liquidated — I have therefore withdrawn the accounts of the afore named Gentlemen and agreeable to your request of this day inclose them to you to be forwarded to the parties — there is an Act of Congress that forecloses all claims on the United States that are not presented by the 28<sup>th</sup> Inst. to advocate this with to give the Claimants an opportunity of receiving their just dues when proper persons are appointed. I have been
39443:
[carefu]ll to have each of the claims voted by a Clerk of the Accountants Office for presentment previous to the Act being in force

I am &<sup>c</sup>
Joseph Howell

Roberts of this City to forward to the proprietors, the principal objection to the settlement appears in the United States not having a party in the Appraisement and that the claims are of too General a Nature to be admitted, as I suppose they may be allowed at some future period. Joseph Howell

Williams produced his Acc<sup>t</sup> done by himself and a very erroneous one to the amount of £104.10.6 the Amount sworn by you he refers to these which was 830 dollars. but know Sir I have no powers to pay before them, therefore advantage will be taken; and perhaps well bring in the Report his amount. which will injure not very much. these Men are to meet 4<sup>th</sup> Inst. a line from you on this Subject will oblige Sir.

W<sup>m</sup> Mos<sup>t</sup> Hble Ser

Joseph Howell Esq<sup>r</sup>          Peter de Places

Office of Army Accounts
New York Aug<sup>t</sup> 7<sup>th</sup> 1788

Gentlemen

The Assistant Commissioners to whom was referred the Petition of Joseph Spence late a Major General in the service of the United States beg leave to report.

That in the Month of November 1777 Gen. Spence received of M<sup>r</sup> Hancock Deputy Master General the sum of 13533 dollars for the use of the department in which he then Commanded; it appears that by a letter from Jonathan Smith Esq. Commissioner that the Sum of 10,382 ¾ dollars of the money was placed to Peter Philips Deputy Commissary General, which leaves a balance of 3000 ¾ dollars to be accounted for by General Spence —

That on the 10<sup>th</sup> June 1775 Congress voted the Additional pay of one hundred and sixty six dollars to a Major General Commanding a separate department.

That General Spence claims this additional pay from the 1<sup>st</sup> January 1777 to the 1<sup>st</sup> April 1779 which time he served he commanded the Army Stationed at West Point and its Vicinity, by order of the late Commander in Chief —

That this claim being admitted (which the Assistant Commissioner is of opinion is proper) his pay for this service will amount to 2432 ⅔ dollars which will still leave a balance due the United States of [undecipherable] dollars, equal at the rate of exchange by the Army Scale to 93 ¾ dollars, then —

That General Spence voluntarily acknowledges that the Monies remaining in his hands was by him returned in March or April 1779 to the Loan Office of the State of Connecticut —

Office of Army Accounts
New York Aug<sup>t</sup> 8<sup>th</sup> 1788

Sir

In reply to your letter of the 19<sup>th</sup> Ulto. to M<sup>r</sup> Pierce, I have to inform you that it is necessary that the monies received by you for the pay of the 2. Regiment Massachusetts should be settled at this Office and whatever balance remains in your hands must be refunded to the same soon for the benefit of the original proprietors.

As you conceive a personal attendance to this business will prejudice your private affairs and will be attended with an expence more than adequate to the Allowance of pay allowed for your services, I shall propose a method whereby you will on a most measure avoid it.

M<sup>r</sup> Swan a Gentleman of this Office proposes a visit to Boston in the course of this Month and has obligingly offered his assistance and if time will permit will formally close your accounts, for which purpose I shall give him authority, in the mean time, I have to request you will prepare your Accounts and put them in a situation that much time may not be required.

I expect M<sup>r</sup> Swan will write you when he is prepared for the business, and as that business may be complicated at a triffling expence on your part, I would suggest the Idea of allowing M<sup>r</sup> Swan some compensation for his kindness and trouble — as soon as M<sup>r</sup> Swan informs me that your accounts are closed and the balance returned
39479:
I will forward the Certificates for your commutation in the manner you may direct.

The Death of J. M<sup>r</sup>. Pierce, which happened on Saturday Morning last oversees this subject, at present the department devolves and will be conducted by me untill Congress are pleased to make a new Appointment.

I am Sir,
Y<sup>r</sup> H<sup>l</sup> Serv<sup>t</sup>.
Joseph Howell

[Missing Page/s]

By the Senate & House of Representatives assembled, September 4, 1788

Resolved, That the Secretary at War be, & he is hereby directed, to have a sufficient number of Troops in the service of the United States in readiness to march from the Ohio to the protection of the Cherokee otherwise honga: Shall direct the same & that he take measures for obtaining information of the Soil limits for troops to march from the Ohio to Chota and for preserving among all the white Inhabitants settled upon or in the vicinity of the hunting Grounds fecured to the Cherokees by the treaty concluded between them & the United States November 28<sup>th</sup> 1785 the proclamation of Congress of this date.

Resolved, That copies of the said proclamation & these resolutions be transmitted to the Executives of Virginia & North Carolina, & that the said States or they are hereby requested to use their influence, that the said proclamation may have its extended effect to excite from & harmoney between a obligations of the United States & the Cherokees, and to prevent any further emigration of their respective subjects from pressing upon Congress, shall find it necessary to order troops to the Cherokee lands to enforce a due observance of the said Treaty. That the said States or they are hereby requested to cooperate with the said Troops for enforcing such observance of that Treaty.

Resolved, That the papers which have been transmitted to Congress concerning certain hostilities alleged to have been committed by John Sevier & others on the Cherokee Indians at Chota, be referred to the Executives of North Carolina, & that the said Executive be, & they are hereby requested to cause enquiry to be made into the said hostilities and to take measures for having the perpetrators thereof apprehended and punished.—

(Signed) Cha Thomson Sec<sup>y</sup>

a true copy from the originals
J. Drayton
39507:
By the United States in Congress Assembled.

A PROCLAMATION.

WHEREAS the United States in Congress assembled, by their Commissioners duly appointed and authorized, did on the twenty-eighth Day of November, One Thousand Seven Hundred and Eighty-five, at Hopewell, on the Keowee, conclude Articles of a Treaty with all the Cherokees; and among other things stipulated and agreed, by Article fourth, "That the Boundary allotted to the Cherokees for their hunting Grounds, between the said Indians and the Citizens of the United States, within the limits of the United States of America, is and shall be the following, viz." Beginning at the mouth of Duck River on the Tennessee; thence running Northerly along the said waters running into Cumberland from those running into the Tenehe; thence Eastwardly along the said ridge to a north-east line to be run, which shall strike the South-Holston thirty-eight miles above Nashville; thence along the said line to the river; thence up the said river to the fork where the Kentucky road cuts the river; thence to Campbell's line near to Cumberland Gap; thence in the mouth of Cloud's Creek on Holstein; thence to the Chimney-Top Mountain; thence to Cany Creek, near the mouth of Big Lime Stone on Nolichuckey; thence a Southerly course six miles to a mountain; thence South to the North-Carolina line; thence to the South-Carolina Indian Boundary; and about the forth South-West over the top of the Oconee Mountain, till it shall strike Tugelo river; thence a direct line to the top of the Currahoe Mountain; thence to the head of the South fork of the Oconee river." And by Article fifth, that "If any Citizen of the United States, or other person not being an Indian, should attempt to settle on any of the lands within and/or South and of the said Boundary, which were allotted to the Indians for their Hunting Grounds, or having settled prior to the conclusion of the said Treaty, and not removing from the same within six months after the ratification of the said Treaty, such person should forfeit the protection of the United States, and that the Indians might punish him as law as they please:" provided, that the said fifth Article should not be construed to exclude settled between the fork of French Broad and Holstein rivers, whose particular situation should be transmitted to the United States in Congress assembled for their decision thereon, which the Indians agreed to abide by." And whereas it has been represented to Congress, that several Persons who have settled on the Frontier of North-Carolina, in the vicinity of Chota, have, in open violation of the said Treaty, made intrusions upon the said Indian Hunting Grounds, and committed many unprovoked outrages upon the said Cherokees, who by the said Treaty have put themselves under the protection of the United States, which proceedings are highly injurious and disrespectful to the authority of the Union, and being the firm determination of Congress to protect the said Cherokees in their rights, according to the true intent and meaning of the said Treaty: THE UNITED STATES IN CONGRESS ASSEMBLED, have therefore thought fit to issue, and they DO hereby issue this their Proclamation, to restrain all Persons from such unconstitutional intrusions, to forbid all proceeding against the said Cherokees, and enjoining all those who have settled upon the said Hunting Grounds of the said Cherokees, to depart with their Families and Effects within such a time, as they shall and may think proper, and before the determination to be expressed in this Resolution as their peril: Provided, that this Proclamation shall not be construed as requiring the removal of the People settled between the fork of French Broad and Holstein rivers, referred to in the said Treaty: Provided also, that nothing contained in this Proclamation shall be considered as affecting the Territorial Claims of the State of North-Carolina.

DONE in Congress, this First Day of September, in the Year of our Lord One Thousand Seven Hundred and Eighty-eight, and of our Sovereignty and Independence the Thirteenth.

CYRUS GRIFFIN, President.

CHARLES THOMSON, Secretary.
39512:
Norwich, 8 & 9 Sept 1801

Dear Sir —

The other day when I was at N York, my tour there was accidentally meeting with unexpected Disappointments, and being in great haste, I returned home. By your attention (to my application by way of Correspondence) Thave no notion that you would wish to treating me, as an individual, as paid, but that you would wish otherwise, that I be Compensated and rewarded —

At the request of Gov<sup>t</sup> Trumbull and Council I commenced in public service of the United States, immediately after the battle of Bunker Hill, attended the wounded there, & Continued through all the burdens of the War — In 1777 April 11<sup>th</sup> was appointed Surg<sup>n</sup> Gen<sup>l</sup> of the Military Hospitals of Congress, & in that Character served till the Arrangement of the Hospital department Octo<sup>r</sup> 7<sup>th</sup> 1780 when I was appointed as Physician & Surgeon, not having the rank and Standing as heretofore; (no wrong, reduced & derogy &c, but at the pleasure of Congress, however the Circumstances, and State of Hospital affairs were such that I continued in actual Service to the 18<sup>th</sup> Jane 1781 when I declined to private practice as p<sup>r</sup> Doc<sup>t</sup> Cochran's certificate intitled to have & receive the same pay, amount of Commutation and emoluments as other Citizens so deranged — As to my letter of the 8<sup>t</sup> June 1781 to Doc<sup>t</sup> Cochran it never entered my heart any such thing as resignation, or any thing looking like it, there is nothing more contained in that letter, than an abse

39514:
[Duplicate]

War Office. September 23<sup>d</sup> 1788

Sir

In the absence of the Secretary at War, I had the honor to write you on [strikethrough: the] 22<sup>d</sup> Ultimo. at the same time enclosed you a resolve of Congress of the 20<sup>th</sup> Ultimo. appointing you Agent for the Chickasaw Nation of Indians the first was addressed to the care of, and to be forwarded by the post master at Richmond, and a duplicate to Hillsborough North Carolina with similar directions.

I now transmit you a resolve of Congress of the 3<sup>d</sup> Instant accompanyng a Proclamation of the same date which is intended to have an extensive circulation, and therefore I take the liberty to enclose you ten copies.

I have the Honor to be
Your Your Most Ob<sup>t</sup> Servant
W<sup>m</sup> Knox

Colonel Joseph Martin
Agent for the Chickasaw & Cherokee
Nations of Indians.
39517:
Hingham Jany 4 1788

My dear Sir

On my return a few days Since from a tour to the eastern part of this common wealth, where I am intrusted in land, and have a few Settled, I recvt your much esteemed favor of the 13<sup>th</sup> of June last. I could not have been more gratified by a line from any friend than from you, who have not only a Sharer with me in the events and fortunes of war, but forgetful of your own Sufferings and Safety was anxious only for mine — Such commiseration even in a heart less forful tells must have made deep impressions than left on my breast were not only deep but remains So and with the remembrance of them I Shall not part untill that period when a new retrospects with prologue Shall be to broken that all we have left with its new all connexion between cause and effect —

I am highly gratified that you have So agreeably connected your Self, So I Shall now feel my Self greatly interested in either ever Shall nearly interest you, the union which you have formed is an honourable one and it will open to you a Source of happiness, which you could not have embraced in the many — may the pleasures of affection

offering them from ever dry up nor its joy, before you and when life Shall expire may you each glide into those Joys in which flow into that Kingdom where you Silently Shall be full and compleat the more they neither marry nor are given in marriage

You offer after Baylies, Rice and my John Baylies and Price yet here but alas! my Son is no more! —

The pain is distressing I write not would your feelings, by dwelling upon the melancholy want.

Your mention that you have been fortunate enough to obtain about 60,000 acres of good land, this acquisition of wealth may lay a foundation for your future happiness as it may enable you to become more extensively useful in the world than without it as it may open to you a door for the full carriage of that Philanthropy which has marked your progress through life. I may however have its temptations and ills, will it not my dear friend betray you into the practice of that Slavery, which has been thought by Some to be justifiable — [strikethrough: it] Should will you be able to justify the matter and vindicate it with those tender feelings those

at all times been alive in your breast and which have always led you not only to Suffer those around you to participate with you in your own joy, but you then from a kind copy and a preference Since more compelled them to partake with your Should you tell me that you are in the practice of Slavery and that you are convinced from the fullest reflection on the Subject that the practice is right I will thank you for these arguments in favour of it which gives conviction and Support to your own mind — Think not Sir that those observations are the language of gloom and misanthrope. I think you know me too well to be offended I think they certainly Spring from a very different Source from a conviction that persons are made capable of enjoying that name of happiness in this life. That those different habitations assigned them, that the benificent author you note they are fitted to the climate in which they are placed and that Heaven in the great Scale is not less anxious as me the perfect State of these, it ever were to be. Although under the former [undecipherable] the inhabitants are more Softly yet and improved art, compared with the inhabitants of colder climes but more

we find little labor and forethought necessary for the earth brings forth Spontaneously most of the necessaries of life the wants of those people are few and are easily Supplied and if left to themselves would be happy — It will not be denied that the African can bear the fervoring rays of the Sun in your climate better than the white inhabitants but this will be urged upon that the inhabitants of Georgia are as able to perform that Share of labour necessary to that climate as well as a New England man can perform the quantity of labour necessary in this far this [undecipherable] trades the quantity is augmented by the former Savings, as there is that Georgia and would be as well approved with, if not better, in Georgia as in the [undecipherable] —

I am much pleased with the account you give of your State I hope a peace on equitable ground might be Settled and that it might have all the advantages of reposing among the States into you Seem to think from its climate and feel it is entitled —

I do not recollect Mr Gibbons I hardly think he was ever a member of my family had any thing very particular to take place while there be on his leaving it I probably think have received from [undecipherable] Y it —

Sir

Since the death of M<sup>r</sup> Brace I have been much at a loss till now to know to whom I was to apply on the subject of a claim which has long since been made by me for commutation and arrears of pay, you I believe must have been acquainted with the Officers of it heretofore, that of establishing from the records of the Commander in Chief that your Brace was not assigned; this M<sup>r</sup> Brace in a letter to me of the 2<sup>d</sup> Octo<sup>r</sup> last, suggested, as the only means whereby he would be justified on the subject, and this by the enclosed which I received the other day from Gen<sup>l</sup> Washington will I think do away all Scruples which have heretofor existed — The resignation of Regimental Commissions took place 16<sup>th</sup> May 1791, previous to my knowledge or your appointment, I shall write the President of Congress on the subject that in case there was no necessity for M<sup>r</sup> Brace, a further matter might be again laid before Congress who have already acted, M<sup>r</sup> Pierce who was not then furnished with the enclosed proof which would effectually have proved him — I have now only myself to request your attention to this affair as briefly as you can and will beg you to drop me a line on the subject by the post. Rec<sup>d</sup> any Barrett will not testify by Doc<sup>r</sup> Buck on the subject must be among the papers of our departed friend which I do thank you to forward me whether you are informed —

A few days since was handed me a letter directed to M<sup>r</sup> Hilldron or Hopkins commissioner for settling army accounts with the Signature of John Thatcher, Kentucky a request that a Certificate might be forwarded him of the time he last received Pay from the United States — on searching into the Books of this Office, I find that their is a Capteain John Thatcher late of Col Smith's Regiment, and commander of the Galey Washington, charged with the sum of 270 ½ dollars being for Cash and Supplies received of the Commissary general of Prisoners while in Captivity — presuming this person who was a prisoner to be the same that I am address, I have to request you to answer the following Queries to my satisfaction and which is absolutely necessary previous to giving the Certificate required —

1<sup>st</sup> where and on what day were you taken Prisoner
2<sup>d</sup> what time were you liberated either by Exchange or parole
3<sup>d</sup> has the State to which you belong advanced you any Monies on account of your Services while a Prisoner
4<sup>th</sup> Have you received any rations, how many, and of whom, since your return from Captivity —

The articles of prisoners on my Books from does not make mention of your name, which makes it necessary that the 1<sup>st</sup> and 2<sup>d</sup> Queries should be particularly answered, and more is done by wit to would Silence satisfactorily — The 3<sup>d</sup> query may be answered by a Certificate from the Commissioner of the Pay Table which I make no doubt you can obtain on application — The 4<sup>th</sup> I am willing to take on your Certificate on honor or otherwise as may be most agreeable — I have been thus particular in order that any obstacle to your request may be removed, and if the State has not made a settlement with you, you will be entitled to me as a

Sir

Your letter of the 24<sup>th</sup> ins<sup>t</sup> is now before me in order to convince you that I have no interest (further than the duty I owe the United States) in retaining stoppages made from you untile your accounts with the United are finally closed. I have taken the trouble to copy the accounts as stated by you with Colonel Wood and have also copied the receipts given by you for monies received of him for the Recruiting Service all of which you will find enclosed. — The monies you have received of Col<sup>o</sup> Wood amount, as by the enclosed copies of receipts to 770 dollars. in your accounts with him I find only the sum of 400 doll<sup>rs</sup> Credited — the amount of your last account against him dated April 23<sup>d</sup> 77 is 333.44<sup>d</sup> this leaves to your debit 4<sup>6</sup> dollars exclusive of the difference between the total received & that which is Credited — the Stoppages of 200 dollars made from you will not even the balance that appears due the United States by 222 dollars. I therefore flatter myself you will see the propriety of my conduct in not complying with your request in issuing a Certificate for the sum retained. & I also expect you will see the necessity of closing this account; which if not done, I am directed to return your name to the Secretary at War as a debtor to the United States, who will in consequence of Directions from Congress retain your warrant for lands. —

[Cap<sup>t</sup> John Marshall }
Lt<sup>t</sup> 5 Reg<sup>t</sup> Penns<sup>a</sup>]   I am Sir & J. Russell Jun<sup>r</sup>

39577:
Comptrollers Office
Hartford October 18, 1782

Sir

In answer to the questions proposed in your Letter of the G<sup>t</sup> instant, you will please to observe, that the depreciation Accounts of the Connecticut Line for those surveys before the 1<sup>st</sup> of January 1776 were settled by Committees of the State of Army, particularly chosen for that purpose, who met & agreed upon the principles of the Settlement before the Act of Congress of the 18<sup>th</sup> of April 1780, was passed — Any information which I therefore undertake to give you will please to consider as being less authentic than what may be derived from the Gentleman who effected the settlement of those Accounts —

These principles were settled by the Committee of the State of Army or by Acts of Assembly — That no person who had left the service otherwise than by death before the 1<sup>st</sup> of January 1776 should be entitled to a settlement —

That all the advances made by the State to the Troops should be charged in the Settlements except what Cloathing had been delivered to them on account of the Continental Allowance —

That the money received by the Troops for their nominal Wages should be charged at a mean value, upon a presumption that it had been received from the United States — which was ascertained in the following manner, and included a compensation for the delay of payment of their Nominal
39580:
Comptrollers Office
Hartford October 6<sup>th</sup> 1788

Sir — In answer to the questions proposed in your letter of the 9<sup>th</sup> instant, you will please to observe, that the Depreciation Accounts of the Connecticut Line for their services before the 1<sup>st</sup> of January 1780 were settled by Committees of the State and Army, particularly chosen for that purpose, who met and agreed upon the principles of the settlement before the Act of Congress of the 16<sup>th</sup> of April 1780, was passed. — Any information which I therefore undertake to give, you will please to consider [undecipherable] being less authentic, than what may be derived from the gentlemen who effected the settlement of those Accounts

These principles were settled by the Committees of State & Army or by Acts of Assembly, That no person who had left the service otherwise than by death before the 1<sup>st</sup> of January 1780, should be entitled to a settlement. —

That all the advances made by the State to the troops should be charged in the settlement
except —

Your letter of the 9<sup>th</sup> Ins<sup>t</sup>. was not received untill saturday last. — The Board of Commissioners have some time since been appointed, but by an act of Congress they cannot enter on the Duties assigned them untill all the accounts are Delivered by the district Commissioners, the same act restrains them to two Clerks only, who are to be taken from the Clerks
39585:
now employed by the Board of Treasury — so that I fear it will not be in the power of M<sup>r</sup> Nourse or of myself to assist you — for myself my friend Richmonds recommendation is sufficient, & I should feel myself happy to see a Gentleman employed who I am sensible merits it — Probably you may come on with the papers, when if an opening should offer you may be assured of my little influence & friendship to serve you —

Joseph Howell

M<sup>r</sup>. John Wright

Office of Army Accounts
New York Oct: 20. 1788

Sir

In reply to your letter of the 8<sup>th</sup> Inst. I have to inform you that I have carefully examined the several returns of the Virginia Lines now in my possession, none of which take any notice of Doctor Brownlie, neither does it appear that he ever applied to M<sup>r</sup>. Dunscomb the Ass<sup>t</sup>. Commissioner for Virginia for a settlement — an application at this day, is too late as the act of Congress of the 2<sup>d</sup>. Novem. 1785, forecloses all army claims that were not presented on or before the 1<sup>st</sup> Aug<sup>t</sup>. 1786 — nothing but a special act of Congress in his favor will justify me in complying with his Demand. I have therefore agreed to your request returned you the papers sent on —

M<sup>r</sup>. Benj. Dawson                                                                           Joseph Howell
39586:
New York, Oct<sup>o</sup> 20<sup>th</sup> 1788

Sir

There is an Edward Whelan in this City, who was formerly a Sergeant in Col<sup>o</sup> John Pattons Regiment, afterwards transferred to the General Guards and discharged from that Corps the 10 March 82 — this poor fellow has been a long time afflicted with Rheumatic and other complaints which prevents him from procuring a Livelihood — I have endeavored to get him on the pension list of this State but without effect — I have therefore to request you will oblige me in the information whether it is now possible for him to Receive any benefit from the State of Pensyl<sup>a</sup> to which he once belonged & from whom he has received his Depreciation — whilst he was in service he was respected as a sober orderly man & a good Soldier — I wish something could be done for him as I conceive he deserves a compensation for the loss of health and the use of limbs in the defence of his Country — I will particularly thank you for a line on this subject as early as possible —

J Howell J.

John Nicholson Esquire

39593:
New York, Oct<sup>o</sup> 22, 1788—

D<sup>r</sup> Sir,

I have begun the examination of the depreciation accounts of the State of Connecticut, and find them as correct as I expected. Smith those of the other States may be found not less so.—

I find on examination of the accounts of the first regiment, that the scale by which you have estimated the mean value of continental money paid to the Troops so nearly corresponds with the scale of Congress that the difference is but trifling. I shall therefore be guided by the one adopted by you.

How far, in justice the State has a right to charge the depreciation, which she has made good to those of her troops that left Service prior to the 10th of April 1780, I do not pretend to determine— The resolution of Congress of that date must however, be my governing principle throughout the whole business.— It will therefore be necessary for me to make the distinction in the course of my examination, for I find that a very considerable part of the Charge exhibited by Connecticut, is for settlements made with such of her troops as are not comprehended within the before mentioned act of Congress.— In this case I shall separate the charge, and take the amount of what has been paid by the State to her troops who continued in service untill after the 10 Apr<sup>l</sup> 1780, as also of what she has paid to those who left service prior to that period and lay the same before

In the first of these you suppose that I am possessed with the printed Register of Certificates issued by him — but this is not the case.

War Office Oct<sup>r</sup> 23. 1788

Sir

I should have written you previous to this period, on the Subject of the money necessary to be advanced for the objects of Lieu<sup>t</sup> Brotts march and requested an order for the Same, but having had reason to expect the arrival of the Secretary at War, either at Hartford or New york by this time. I have delayed writing you.

The clothing for the Regiments being to go on with Lieu<sup>t</sup> Bratt will require a different arrangement with respect to the amount of Money to be advanced, from what would have been wanted for the transportation of the baggage of this party only — The Contract for the Clothing having been formed Subsequent to Gen<sup>l</sup> Knox departure from this City, no directions Stipulating the amount necessary for the transportation of the Same to the frontiers, were left by him; I am therefore induced, to wait his further instructions on this Subject — He arrived at Boston on the evening of the 16<sup>th</sup> from the Eastward, and this nights post will most probably bring answers to my letters on this Subject, or he will be on to Hartford in a day or two himself.

M<sup>r</sup> Bratt informs in a letter of the 19<sup>th</sup> Ins<sup>t</sup>. that his business is completed at Hartford, and that he Shall march on the 22<sup>d</sup> for West Point.

I take the liberty of addressing a letter for Gen<sup>l</sup> Knox to your care. In whose absence.

I have the Honor to be
Sir, with great Respect
Your Most Obedient Servant
W<sup>m</sup> Knox,

The Hon<sup>ble</sup>
Jeremiah Wadsworth Esq<sup>re</sup>

Baltimore Oct<sup>r</sup> 25. 1798

Dear Sir

Capt. Simon White, the bearer of this is a particular friend of mine; whose Schooner is a constant Packet between this place and New York, any favor you may please to render him at any time, shall be considered as a continued acknowledgment to your friend —

My brother Benjamin some time since informed me that on a further investigation of the balance due me there appeared only 700. yrs — Dollies in place of 900 dollrs as at first stated; as I am sensible of your accuracy in those things I take it for granted it must be so, have therefore to request that you will please deliver a certificate for the same to Cap<sup>t</sup> White, as he will produce an Order from me for the purpose

I condole with you my good friend, on the public & private loss of the once worthy M<sup>r</sup> Pierce, excuse me when I say that I do not know where the public can supply his place by a character more suitable than yourself — I speak the sentiments of my heart

Do write me by the return of Cap<sup>t</sup> White I shall esteem it a particular favor conferred on an old friend — please remember affectionately to brother Boy — inform him I wrote about a week or ten days since, which letter I hope he rec<sup>d</sup>

I am Dear Sir with every sentiment esteem
and regard
yours
Sincerely
In<sup>o</sup> Bankson

mine of the 2<sup>d</sup> I gave you all the information relative to Price & Burke that was then in my power to give you — I have not yet heard any thing of Bird from West Point from which I am fearful it is not in my power to furnish to Harpers the stock hand it would be necessary to be at Brunswick — Would it not have been better for M<sup>r</sup>. Bray who did the Inspects for Higher Satisfactions, to have transported the Baggage and Clothing from Brunswick to Trenton ferry and for the waggons engaged by Harpers to take them there? — Cap<sup>t</sup>. Price says four waggons will not be sufficient for the Clothing however we paid for them — He talks about having the Clothing 16 complete fully being embarked on a Brigg, an impossibility to foresee the Hats without engraving them — Cap<sup>t</sup> Price will visit West Point this evening or tomorrow morning by last boats, he also heard from Pratt and Leavy Hartfords until last Saturday that he could get perfectly equipped and leave the points until Monday next. Hartford Novem<sup>br</sup> 3. 1788.

Sir,

I have recieved your favour of the 22. of Oct last relative to the depreciation accounts of this State and finds that your institution of the Act of Congress of the 10<sup>th</sup> of April 1785 is such as I expected, though I have no doubt but that claims upon the Union as equitable as those of Connecticut will be finally admitted —

I now inclose copies of sundry Acts of the General Assembly and Governor & Council of Safety, to enable you to form an idea of the engagements entered into by this State to their Logs of the Army and of the mode by which they have been fulfilled — By these Acts you will percieve that immediately, upon Raising the Army, the State undertook to Supply cloathing and refreshments at specie prices which were delivered by State Commissaries appointted for that purpose and that the families of officers and soldiers were also furnished with supplies at a stated price upon depositing a sum in nominal money equal to the specie value of the supplies which they were entitled to receive — the expenditures for these purposes reduced by the reales

V<sup>iz</sup>. one rec<sup>t</sup> for 2 months pay and one rec<sup>t</sup> for one months pay recvd from Col<sup>o</sup> Trumbull — also one other rec<sup>t</sup> to Mare & Colwell for Two thousand doll<sup>rs</sup> in which it is presumed the remainder of Dunlaps pay was included — I would wish Capt Howal to mention particularly as he did to me that I cannot receive any pay from the public untill the last mentioned rec<sup>t</sup> is settled by Abram Smith, then Cap<sup>t</sup> of the Regiment — and who had the setting of the acc<sup>ts</sup> —

J<sup>no</sup> Dunlap
39627:
New york 5<sup>th</sup> Nov<sup>r</sup> 1788

My dear Sir,

I have this moment received your favor of the 2<sup>d</sup> with an enclosure for M<sup>rs</sup> Greene, but She left, last Tuesday afternoon for Bethlehem— Do you wish me to retain it until She returns, or can you suppose it will reach her by being forwarded to any place?

D<sup>r</sup> Pratt arrived here yesterday from W Point on his way to N Brunswick by water from which latter place he will take charge of a quantity of cloathes for the troops serving on the frontiers— He has Hitherto been a very faithfull and active Officer, he left this City in the afternoon with an assurance from me that if I did not hear from Gen<sup>l</sup> Knox by this evenings post, relative to some additional supply to him of Cash, that I would undertake to allow him an additional months pay, with which prospect he has gone to Brunswicks to which place Cap<sup>t</sup> Hogg will repairs in order to muster & inspect his troops, and make him the advances for the objects of his march.

I beg you to tender my best Complements to M<sup>rs</sup> Wadsworth, your fair Young ladies and little Miss Genkens, that we are all anxious to have her return again to N York and that she must not fail to hold herself in readiness to not embrace the opportunity of coming with my brother & Niece, who will certainly leave Boston next Week—

Pray frank your letters when you do me the Honor to write, or address under cover to Gen<sup>l</sup> Knox, those post Office people have charged me [undecipherable] for letters I have rec<sup>d</sup> from [undecipherable] on public Service — they are however half right on this subject.

I am with Sincere Attachment
My dear Sir
Your very humb Serv<sup>t</sup>
Wm Knox

Col. Wadsworth.

[address leaf: The Hon<sup>ble</sup> Jeremiah Wadsworth Esq<sup>r</sup> / Hartford / Connecticut]
39628:
Office of Army Accounts
New York Nov<sup>r</sup> 5. 1788

Sir

Your note of the 3<sup>d</sup> Inst<sup>t</sup> covering a memorandum from Major Dunlaps were received this Morning.

I have now before me the account of Cap<sup>t</sup> Abraham Smith as Settled by the commissioners of the Treasury in May 1777 — wherein it appears that the several sums of money received by Cap<sup>t</sup> Smith for the pay and recruiting of the old 6 regiment are accounted for: it appears by this account that the Regiment was fully Settled with for their pay to the 1 Jan<sup>y</sup> 1777 & that the 2000 dollars rec<sup>d</sup> by Cap<sup>t</sup> Smith in Philadel<sup>a</sup> was in part pay for the month of Jan<sup>y</sup>. which sum he distributed to the several Captains, no part of this sum appears to have been for Major Dunlaps altho' he appears to have been in the estimate for pay to the 1 Jan<sup>y</sup> by the account as it now stands was paid to that time tho' no receipts appears either from him or from the other officers — I suppose the commissioners were Satisfied at the time, that the charges were proper. — To investigate the whole of this transaction would take some days, I have therefore given you the heads which perhaps may answer my purpose — the residue of pay appearing due Major Dunlaps I am willing to admit provided he made the claim before on or before the 1 Aug<sup>t</sup> 1785 — the post being nearly ready will prevent my enlarging on this Subject.

Joseph Howell

Doc<sup>tt</sup> Robert Johnson

Sir

I have the Honor to acquaint you the person referred out by Atty. Allen for Copying go. has been Aplaced, Except the Musket Cartridges which Remain yet to be accounted for. The Cartridges Shall be Shiped by the next Packet agreeable to your order when at Providence. Capt Brown has a few tonight or Shall have had them by him.

This State have again Refused to appoint a Convention for Considering the New Constitution; in the legal move the Question was put on Saturday last on the [undecipherable] of Delegates for a Convention, when after much Debate it was negatived by a Majority of three to one — after which (M<sup>r</sup> Hazard are Implaceable & potent Enemy to the New System & his Decking themselves in all the Vile Politic Campaigns in this Devoted State) brought forward a motion for Printing Copies of the Circular Letter from the New York Convention throughout this State — & Submitting to [undecipherable] the People at large the Propriety of Signing Delegates to meet a proposed Convention for Considering [undecipherable] agreeable to the [undecipherable] of the afore [undecipherable] Letter — which being part after [undecipherable] Debate by allowing three to one in favor of the Motion —
39631:
New York 5<sup>th</sup> November 1788

Lieu<sup>t</sup> Pratt arrived here yesterday on his way from West Point to New Brunswick and left it in the afternoon, after having received on board his vessel the Ten Tents, and receiving a lest & drum from Mr Hagg. — His wants were expressed so Strongly and which he said he y Col<sup>o</sup> Wadsworth had convoyed to That I thought it unavoidable to promise him another month's pay (making ten for himself) if I should not hear from you by the post of this evening on that subject — he appeared much relieved by this promise and said Should it not meet your approbation, that the same might be stopped from what he Should next receive from Blanks.

Mr Pratt will receive a months pay for four privates more than your estimate mentions, being old Soldiers who have joined him — chadpen has written that he would procure them & I hope, there will be no wanting for them any, it can be but Pratt, having lent the on the dimensions of the Boxes, he says Jas will take them all — We have been obliged to Jens to McLafer also for Cartridge Boxes and several Small things with which Pratt did not supply himself at the Point — He has had him Almost Amusing the Deputies from Hagg, who was tried and [undecipherable] at the Point.

[Mr K. Bliss]   New York 9<sup>th</sup> Nov<sup>r</sup> 1788

Your favor of the 2<sup>d</sup> Inst came duly to hand— Lieut Brett and the clothing are arrived safe at Brunswick— he reached that place on the 6<sup>th</sup> Mr Hagg was there the 7<sup>th</sup> and the waggons from Pennsylvania arrived the same day however the artillery which S<sup>t</sup> Hagg wrote for to Mr Hepburn on the 5<sup>th</sup> had not arrived at Brunswick, but probably came there by the Haze on Saturday— Mr Hagg expected to muster & Inspect the troops on the 9<sup>th</sup> and to march them tomorrow the 10<sup>th</sup>

Mr Hodgdon's agreement with the Waggoners is expressed in his letter of the 5<sup>th</sup> to Mr Hagg as follows

"This morning I rec<sup>d</sup> your favor of the 3 Instant. The Seven Waggons are engaged under the following orders I they will inform you with this— The conditions of these agreements are as follows - Viz each Waggon to be paid for at the rate of Twenty Jerseys Penn<sup>a</sup> Currency per day, to commence on the day they leave this City to go to Brunswick - to load with a Ton weight Gross each if required, to proceed with the troops - to receive Twenty pounds each in advance, and to be allowed thirteen days to return to their respective homes"

I hope this agreement will meet with your approbation. Hodgdon is accustomed to that business, and probably has done as well as any person could do in a similar case.

Mr Brett will undoubtedly leave Brunswick tomorrow if the weather is fair— I'm in [undecipherable] this City represented his [undecipherable]

39651:
[A Talk from the headmen and Warriors of the Cherokee Nation at a meeting held at Ustinare the beloved Town the 20 November 1788 Addressed to the Honourable Richard Winn Esqre Superintendent and for the Southern department in answer to a Talk sent by him dated the 12 Oct 1788]

Freind & Brother

We received your Talk likewise the Resolves of Congress dated 1 Sep 1788 likewise a Copy of a Letter from the Governor of North Carolina to you and the proclamation from Congress, all which affords us much satisfaction that we have on your and our friends who tells us the truth and endeavours to do us Justice it likewise gives us much satisfaction to hear from Congress and Virginia

Brother

You have opened our Eye and likewise our hearts the Talks we receive from you pleases us much that Congress is determined to have our hunting Grounds open so that our young men may hunt and will also to purchase Goods of our traders to Cloath our selves and furnishen our hunts; Grounds was very severely viewed gives us the greatest satisfaction that it will be soon enlarged as appears by the proclamation from Congress it likewise gives us much satisfaction that we have a view of returning from the Woods where we have been drove and once more sitting again in our old Towns which we wished to do When we are certain that the white people have quited our hunting Grounds —

Brother

It affords us much satisfaction that a friendly sick will soon take place you informs us if you have wrot to the Governor of North Carolina to fix a time and place for that purpose as at that time we will talk over all matters and smoke the pipe of friendship.

The headman and Warriors from the middle towns was on their way to Ustinare but being informed that it was good Talks and that white Birds and Tobacco was sent from Ustinare to all the Towns in the Nation they went back fearing that some of their Young men might go out again and as one of the head men are determined to put a stop to all Hostility and for the time to come live like Brothers and friends as long as the sun shines and water runs

The following Talk comes from the Little Turkey —

Freind & Brother

Your talk I have heard which gives us the greatest satisfaction likewise all our beloved men in my part of the Nation it is a Talk from you our great beloved Brother who thou informed is appointed by Congress to see Justice done us we have now heard from our beloved Brothers from New York likewise Virginia which now opens our Eye and hearts for they are the men that we must abide by your Talks are good and your friendship we look on ensures for the good and your land, I have seen the Resolves of Congress likewise the proclamation for all the white people Settled on our hunting Grounds to go of without —

loss of time.

Freind & Brother

I have the satisfaction to inform you that Alexander McGilvery chief of the Creek Nation has taken your Talks likewise the Talks of from Congress and Virginia and means to hold them fast and when they meet will take his white Brothers by the hand as well and Those live in peace and friendship as long as the Grass grows and the water runs —

A Copy of this Talk you will please to send to Congress and Virginia it will be the more better every thing can be sifted to your entire satisfaction — because some of your beloved men are out hunting as for the present it is impossible for us to send them to Ustinare at this time because they are scattered through the Nation but they shall be restored to their friends as soon as possible we shall have them all collected together Orders are given out that they may be with that my people should not be restied upon hereafter for us their prisoners all they will be oblige to acquaint their friends and hope they will make themselves easy for a short time.

Freind and Brother

We must inform you that we look upon the white people that [strikethrough: is] on the New State very deceitful we have experienced them, and are much afraid of them we are now oblige to hub Spring out continually on the frontiers fearing they will Return and do as an [undecipherable] as they have before —

Freind & Brother

We must inform you that there is some Cherokee out now and some few of our people that are not yet come in if any mischief should be done it is contrary to our desire but on their Return will all be Settled and all Hostility cease against the white people and the faith made while.

We must inform you that several Talks that has been [strikethrough: at] before the headmen and Warriors at Ustinare has been opened before we received that your last Talk came under Cover to M<sup>r</sup> Grigg and by him delivered in the Square at Ustinare to our beloved men, we do not approv of any person opening any Talks that comes from our white Brothers yacht M<sup>r</sup> Grigg who delivers them to us or our Linguist James Carry

The Boy we had prisioner at Coosawattee we are informed is delivered to Upper Spain in order that he may be carried down to Pensicola the Girl is not as yet come to his friends but we presume She is in the land

We now have finished our Talk and in Token of peace and friendship we have inclosed a Sprig of white wampum —

The Little Turkey — Yallow Bird — Chickasawlee — Dick
Killygushee — Shigh — Councillors — Blaze
Badger — Island man — Dragon Chained — [undecipherable]
The Tollerson — Alchesemath — Notawaha — [undecipherable]
The Warrior Nekhau — Bear — Hanging man — [undecipherable]
out of the Hole — Walls — Nutley
Footwaird — [undecipherable]

New York Nov 21 1700

Sir,

Your letter of the 31 Ult<sup>o</sup> was handed me yesterday, & agreeably to your request I have diligently Searched every Account and Muster Roll that could lead me to the information required by you, and cannot find Prince Negro further than the first of Jany 1782 from which time he appears to have been omitted. — As I have a wish to serve every good Soldier who has served his country, and particularly those who have been maimed in the Service I have to request you will inform me as soon as possible what regiment Prince Negro was in at the time he was transferred & who commanded the time he was transferred — at what time he was allowed to go home by Col<sup>o</sup> Durkee & who was his Captain from time to time, also whether he ever joined the Invalid regiment these facts being ascertained will enable me to trace him & give the information required I shall inclose this to Oliver Wolcott esq<sup>r</sup> at Hartford who I request will forward it immediately that the time may not elapse before I hear from you

J. Howell Jr.

Capt. Moses Cleaveland

M J Howell.     Philadelphia Dec 4. 1700

Sir

Pleased to inform me if this Congress has or ever will pass an act for settling my accounts. — was obliged to pay to M<sup>r</sup> Williams two hundred and twenty pounds in specie which I thought very wrong — Shall be for ever oblig'd for your effort.

[N.B. this letter should have been inserted before that on the pregoing page. —]

B de Haas

To J Howell Esq<sup>r</sup>     Philadel. 30 Nov<sup>r</sup> 1700.

Sir     It is some time since I rec<sup>d</sup> yours with one indeed from Bigham and Should sooner have acknowledged it had I not been very ill with a severe cold attended with a fever from which I am thank God recovered —

While I lived in Pottstown, a Charles Washing-ton of the Invalid reg<sup>t</sup> took out a writ against me for his arrears of pay, but Freedom come to town and when I had staid but a short time, the Sheriff returned it now est in enter. As I may be exposed to the like from other wrong heads & have not anything to shew that I have Settled my accounts & paid the balance into the hands of the Pay Master Gen<sup>l</sup>. I must request you will send me one which will much oblige

Yours &c
Lewis Nicolas
39674:
[from 82668 to 82668. for pay and fulleft & Commutation of Cap<sup>t</sup> Dorsey
" 82669 to 82675 for pay and fulleft & commutation of J<sup>t</sup> Findley
" 82602. for pay & Subsistence of R. C. Skerrell]

Office of Army Accounts
New York Dec 8. 1788 —

Sir

In answer to your letter of the 4 In<sup>t</sup> I have to inform you that there is not a sufficient number of members to form a Congress & probably there will not be untill the new Government shall be in operation. I have done as much as lays in my power to the settlement of your late fathers accounts and lament that his inattention to his own interest as well as the public should be a disadvantage to you. — It is a great misfortune that receipts have not been taken from the officers, as those who are still alive may have it in their power to deny the charge, and the heirs of those who are dead, for the same reason cannot be called on to a settlement. — the accounts being referred to the Board of Treasury cannot be further acted on by me unless by their order or that of Congress — I flatter myself no other officer will have it in his power to lay a similar claim with that of Col<sup>o</sup> Williamson, but if it should be the case I will with pleasure give you every assistance consistant with my official capacity.

J Howell Jr.

M<sup>r</sup> John P De Haas

39678:
[(Copy)]

Providence 8<sup>th</sup> Decem<sup>r</sup> 1788.

I conceive it my duty to inform you that on the Morning of the 5<sup>th</sup> Inst. I discovered the Magazine (containing the Powder & [strikethrough: ball] and ammunition in this Town.) had been break open by some daring Villains, an examination from Happy to find only three Casks, containing containing one hundred pounds, one half — the remainder was very modest indeed after having the temerity to make forcible Entry into the Magazine by digging out two Tier of Brick near the Window of the Surred they thereat which hole they must have been there, a very deft — the Tricks that Leaves the Shelter after they have taking that powder they bowed they boated the Shutting & Replaced the Brick again as low order. No Efforts to recover the Thing — I had the most immediate Alarmed & have secured the Matter with a had Seal on the Brick & Lime mixed over the Whole which will Render it tolerable. I Shall to be opened should I happen to attend the February in his Stay in effect Shared by double Doors of Stone-Locks —

I have taken the Necessary Steps for informing the Towns having communicated the matter to the first Keeper in the Town that Station Renders who will inform any threats any System of Defensive character of the army for Sale — I have ordered this notice to a public Advertisement so that would most Probably Suit The Villains now on their Guard —

at the last Annual Meeting of the Rhode Island Society of Cincinnatti — Towns Affairs was admitted as member for whom Diplomas are wanted; Should you have any on hand Signed by the Assistant General, I Request you will be so good as to forward [strikethrough: him] same with the price, by the first Safe Conveyance for which I will be accountable —

I have the Honor to be with Sentiments of Esteem S<sup>r</sup> y<sup>r</sup> Obed. Humb<sup>l</sup> Serv<sup>t</sup>

Jerv<sup>t</sup>. Olney —

[(Copy) Letter to the Secretary at War. Decem<sup>r</sup> 8. 1788.]

From<sup>e</sup> the Maj<sup>r</sup> Gen<sup>l</sup> Knox —
Secretary at War —

You will find from the within papers that I now by have to intercede for a faithful and brave foldier, whose claims remains unadjusted. The same little favor — to the papers I refer you, and will only add, that the claim was presented me as mentioned, that had it been lodged from July to August would have been allowed to send it forward, the failure not being seen in time prevented its being again presented and sent — upon the whole from the merits of the claim — the real intention of limitation, & your disposition to do justice particularly to a faithful foldier, I trust you can very readily dispense with the rule of Office, and consider this claim entitled to notice; and issue your certificates accordingly so to the passing a receipt, by the papers Col<sup>t</sup> Gamble is vested with a power, and which he has turned over to me. I would therefore beg the favor of any Gentlemen in your Office to sign the receipt and this letter may be filed as authority for so doing & if you to send the same to me that I may forward them to the foldier —

If this will not do please to inform me the requisites and I will write for them to be furnished —

I am with real regard
your friend
A. Dunscomb

Sir —

In answer to your request of "how long it wo'd take
to make out a regimental account" I have to observe that
a person acquainted with the nature of army accounts
would take but a little time compared with that of another
uninformed in the buisness, besides it is to be further known
that it greatly depends upon the accounts themselves as
some are much more complex than others and to decide
on this question a person should be acquainted with
the very accounts alluded to —

The accounts of your late father were (to
the best of my recollection) examined & filed by me in about
four weeks —

J. Howell J<sup>r</sup>
